Not the type to break defenders down with the dribble. Positional rebounder. Defending is an area of concern.

Has a slender build but with a long wing span. Has quick feet and hands and speed with the basketball but is not a powerful nor explosive athlete. Competitive and plays with energy. Good shooter from three both off the catch and off the dribble. Likes to get to the basket. Has room to develop his scoring in the midrange. Is a willing and early passer. Gives the ball up with timing. Dangerous defender. Uses his hands extremely well defensive disruptor. Has potential as a guard rebounder with his competitive instincts and sure hands.

Tremendous physical build. Ultra-athletic with a blend of power and elusiveness. Highly competitive with a strong motor. Loves to attack the rim and the ball with physical abandonment. Improving as a shooter. Has a tad bit mechanical release through the middle of his face. Shoots with a high arc and good rotation. Likes to size up his shot. Will shoot the ball plenty well enough with his other skills. Can attack rim either left or right. For his power, has surprisingly nimble feet on the drive and can side step charges and make awkward finishes. Does have the tendency to force action and pick up turnovers. Handles ball with power and aggression. Sees the floor and can deliver quality passes. Dangerous rebounder. Is a versatile and dominant defender. Capable of guarding every position.

Haggerty has been one of the most prolific guards in college basketball for three straight seasons. He averaged 23.5 points, 5.3 rebounds, and 3.8 assists last season for Kansas State on 49/35/70 shooting splits. The year prior he was the AAC Player of the Year at Memphis after putting up nearly 22 points, 6 rebounds, and 4 assists on 48/36/82 shooting. Before that he was the AAC Freshman of the Year at Tulsa with averages of 21, 6, and 4 on 49/29/77 shooting. At Texas A&M, we may see his best season yet. The Aggies thrive in transition, ranking 16th overall in offensive tempo last season. The only caveat is that while Haggerty may get more total possessions, it will likely be in fewer minutes as he has averaged 33 or more minutes for three straight seasons and Bucky McMillan played no one on his roster more than an average of 26 per night last season. Haggerty is a versatile, volume scorer who can put points on the board in a number of ways. While he can be very effective in the open court, he also knows how to score when the game slows down. He's good enough with the ball to play some point, but is probably most potent off the ball, rating in the 97th percentile on cuts and 88th off screens per synergy. He's also very adept at drawing fouls, and getting easy point from the free-throw line. Combine that with good length, a real nose for the ball as a perimeter rebounder, and the fact that he'll be 22 years old and Texas A&M just landed one of the best guards in the portal.

Agarwal is a deadeye shooter off the catch. He excels at spotting up in transition, coming off screens, and finding openings based on penetration reads. One part of his game that usually goes unnoticed is his basketball IQ. Agarwal specializes as a shooter but he has a generally good understanding of how to play with other good players on the offensive end. He is tougher than most give him credit for and will mix it up and compete on the glass. His glaring weakness will be his body as he needs to add weight to his frail and gangly frame. Although a bit streaky at times, Agarwal will be a sharpshooting forward who should fit nicely into offensive structure and provide as a floor spacer at the next level.

Byrd is a long and athletic wing who stands out on the defensive end of the floor. Standing at 6-foot-6 with a 6-foot-10 wingspan, he's a fluid mover with good two-way instincts who can guard the opposition's best perimeter scorer on a nightly basis. He's also a playmaker on that end, who averaged over 3 stocks per game in each of the last two seasons (1.9 steals and 1.2 blocks in 2025-26) and is a good wing rebounder as well. Offensively, he's been unable to make the jump that NBA scouts have hoped for. The southpaw is an inconsistent shooter, who made just under 31% of his threes last year, on over 4 attempts per game. He has soft enough natural touch, as evidenced by being an 82% career free-throw shooter, but lacks a repeatable release from long range and tends to spray his misses as a result. While he can struggle to score, he has some flashes as a passer, cutter, and open-floor athlete. He's definitely best-suited for an up-tempo system.

Has tremendous size for a point guard. At 6-5 with broad shoulders will be capable of playing the one through the three. Is a smooth athlete who is comfortable with the basketball. More of a straight ahead athlete than a lateral athlete. Plays too upright on the defensive end. Can score at all three levels. Shoots well off the dribble in the midrange and has an array of finishes. Scores with strength and elusiveness around the basket. His long range shooting is developing but he shoots it well enough to keep the defense honest. Is a confident and efficient ball handler. Sees the floor and plays with a high IQ and a poised pace. Has a quality balance between scoring and distributing. Could assert himself more as a rebounder. Has versatility defensively but is a positional defender and not a pressure defender. Point guard quickness could give him problems.

Robbie Avila is a 6-foot-10 big man with extreme skill, craftiness, and basketball intelligence. He’s an inside-out scoring threat and also an advanced passer, but lacks the athleticism or body composition of most college stars, leading to the frequent references as the mid-major Jokic last year at Indiana State. What makes Avila such a difficult defensive cover is his offensive versatility and consequent ability to punish any coverage. Inside the lane, he has terrific hands, footwork, and an understanding of how to seal. While he’s rarely an above the rim finisher, he’s an exceptionally quick one, who is equally good with both hands and thus able to convert 69% of his attempts. He’s also a very good dribbler who sees the floor waiting on secondary defenders in order to utilize his passing ability. On the perimeter, he’s a consistent three-point shooter, both in pick-and-pop action as well as spacing the floor. He ranked in the 89th percentile in catch-and-shoot situations last season per Synergy Sports and had total shooting splits of 54% from the floor, 39% from three, and 81% from the free-throw line. He’s again crafty with his dribble, able to attack bad close-outs, create angles with his footwork, and spin off defenders on his way to the paint. He can also play out of short-rolls and reward cutters as a facilitator from the elbow, top of the key, and potentially any other spot on the floor. The biggest questions are on the defensive end given his lack of speed or athleticism, and while there could be limitations that become more apparent at a higher level, his overall awareness and intelligence helps him compensate for what he may lack physically. Nevertheless, he isn’t much of a shot-blocking threat, but gets his hands on some loose balls while rebounding his area.

Ejiofor is an athletic physical force to be reckoned with. He is strong, productive on the glass, talented, and increasingly versatile offensively from 20 feet and in. His versatility is in his offensive approach. He likes to post up or face up and attack to score through contact. Ejiofor has a college-ready body in which he relies on physicality to compensate against others who are taller and longer. He still has ways to go in terms of adding functional strength and struggles guarding in space. He additionally needs polish as he lacks feel for the game but is a blank canvas when looking at his offensive skillset. Once he learns how to be effective in the screen and roll game, his production should increase tremendously.

Hornecker is a 6-foot-11 big man who has a natural element of touch to his game that should continue to develop. Learning the art of the post-up game will take time but when he understands it and can score over like size he will become a decent low post scorer. His glaring weakness comes in terms of his everyday passion to play the game as he needs to become more assertive in seeking the ball off the rim and protecting it. However, his size and skill give him the potential to be a productive collegiate big man down the road.

A McDonald's All-American coming out of high school, Ernest Udeh doesn't score a lot but he still provides a ton of value. A space-eating big man who runs the court well enough, he is a high-volume rebounder who has averaged over 11 rebounds per 40 minutes throughout his career. A reliable finisher who embraces being a physical big man, Udeh has made over 65% of his field goals as a college player.

Arceneaux is an explosive athlete with a wiry build. He is a long and rangy prospect who gets off his feet to rise up in a hurry and make exciting plays above the rim. He stands out on the defensive end with his excellent wingspan and overall athleticism. Each time he laces them up, Arceneaux is bound to get his fair share of deflections and blocks. He is an elite rim protector who blocks shots in the most exciting fashion due to his bounce and timing. He has a lot of upside and a reason to bet on it is because of how much his overall skill set has improved throughout the years. He has proven to be a consistent open shooter from behind the arc and is a good enough ball handler to play off ball screens and be reliable secondarily. He has not been looked upon as a go-to type scorer or playmaker but he is a one who shines in big moments and has the tools and potential that translate to the highest levels of competition.

Has positive length for a small forward wo can also play as a stretch four. Expect him to play both positions in college. Is best in open space where he can use his dribbling skills to get a head of steam to the rim. Not a physical player so is not the same type of athlete and playmaker in traffic with contact. Shots the ball well from beyond the arc and projects to continue to improve in that area. Likes to attack with the right hand drive and the pull up when going left. Is a crafty finisher in the lane. Can be a secondary ball handler and has court vision as a passer. Can rebound outside his area and chase down a rebound. Struggles defensively and on the boards against physical athletes. Tends to play with too high a center of gravity. Can get into passing lanes defensively. Has disruptive length and athleticism.

Pushing 6-foot-9, Jackson has an ideal basketball frame. He's got long arms and a wiry build that will allow him to add needed strength without costing him athleticism and make no mistake, athleticism is a big part of his game. Jackson is able to fly around the court thanks to his ability to accelerate, change directions and get off the floor in a hurry in tight spaces. Though he still has to get stronger, his athleticism is all functional and he's able to play through contact and doesn't need a runway in order to take off. While his athleticism is an important part of his game and what helps to separate him from the pack, he doesn't have to rely on it. Over the last year, Jackson has made significant improvements when it comes to his skill level. He is a more than capable jump shooter, has a nice jump hook and he's become a real threat to face up and attack off the dribble. His passing has some room for growth but he's improved greatly when it comes to recognizing double teams and finding an open teammate. On the block he has an advantage because of his quickness and while his perimeter ability is what may allow him to create distance between himself and other big men in the class, he has to be careful not to float around out there too much. When he's got an even mix of inside and outside to go along with what he's able to do running the floor in transition, Jackson is a major problem on the offensive end. Defensively, he's ahead of the curve as well. He's got great anticipation, moves well laterally and uses his athleticism to his advantage so that he's an above average rim protector who can also defend in space if he gets caught on a switch and has to defend a smaller player.

Traore is a skilled, efficient, and productive big man with a good natural frame. His efficiency comes in how he generally plays within himself offensively and does what is asked of him on the floor. Traore displays a natural shooting touch not only inside but out to 15 feet and he always looks dunk everything he possibly can while operating inside or in the dunker spot. He also has great hands, is a solid rebounder, and runs the floor with purpose. Traore has the size, mobility, and skill as a big man that high major guards would love to play with.

Corhen is a talented faceup big man prospect with a solid combination of size and skill, specifically his shooting accuracy that extends to the three-point line. His touch from deep can be his kryptonite at times as he tends to float around the perimeter instead of looking to post up within 15-17 feet to show his entire package offensively. Corhen plays with a tremendous amount of effort on the floor. His hands have looked far better in recent months as opposed to when he first entered high school as he has shown flashes of making tough catches, both with off-target passes as well as while running full speed. He will need to continue to add weight and bulk up to physically compete at a power conference school but he is an inside-out big man prospect with a great amount of upside due to his size, touch, and great work ethic.

Has good length for a wing. Has the skill package and strength to play as a small ball four-man. Solid athlete who thrives on his strength and savvy to make plays in traffic. The lefty can stretch the defense with his quick release three-point shooting. Not a big off the dribble scorer but can penetrate defenses and finish but more importantly can find teammates. Plays with poised control and sees the game. Has a sure handle. Smart defender with versatility and an understanding of positioning although not overly quick. Rebounds well with steady hands.

Has a top notch physique with strength and athleticism. Runs like a deer and is an explosive leaper. Has good hands. Finishes lobs with authority and is a high level rebounder. Raw skill wise but shows promise as an attack the basket off a couple dribbles threat and as a respectable jump shooter. Defends and rebounds with passion. Can protect the rim.

A well built, powerful athlete who has the frame to become even more imposing physically. A top notch athlete who plays with a relentless motor. Outside shooting is a weakness, but improvement in that area is expected. Has an explosive first step and loves to attack the rim. Also has a good post up game. Handles the ball adequately in the open court and will see the pass but isn't considered a playmaker for others. Is more of a finisher. Outstanding rebounder. Can control space and also chase down boards outside his area. Is a remarkable shot blocker for his size. Has the defensive versatility to virtually defend every position on the court.

AJ Storr had a lost junior season at Kansas, but it's hard to forget the moments of brilliance that he had at Wisconsin in 2023-24 when he looked like a potential next-level player. Storr offers a bit of appeal as a potential buy-low option for wing-needy teams who need a jolt of scoring. At his best, Storr can provide a three-level scorer who can bulldoze his way to 15 points a night. But he's never shown much promise as a playmaker and his attention to detail defensively is iffy at best. Storr is also well-traveled for a reason which could scare off some staffs, but the upside is clear.

Is thin but has the frame to put on weight. Is a long 6-9/6-10. Moves his feet well and has good hands. Is a mobile and good athlete who is developing the strength to make more plays in traffic. Has three-point shooting range and can finish post moves with either hand. Has a go-to move in the post with his right hand jump hook. Catches well on the move. Built for pick and pop and pick and roll situations. Not a big ball handler but can attack the basket with a couple dribbles. Has a quick first step/dribble. Not a creative passer but has the awareness to make the proper read. Can defend the basket but needs to get stronger to control space down low. Has the lateral quickness to defend ball screen situations. Will go and get a rebound, but can be out muscled. Has a ton of upside. Strength gain is the key.

Jennings is a strong and athletic four-man who is at his best when he plays with a great motor and looks to make plays at the rim. He sprints the floor and finishes with power and flare above the rim. He understands the value of playing hard on a consistent basis but needs to consistently stay motored up in all showings. He also becomes infatuated with the three-point shot at times and while he is an average and capable shooter from distance, he is more effective operating inside the arc. Jennings has great upside and has a chance at being a no-brainer high major prospect with continued skill development.

A strong point guard with a powerful physique. Has a physical game on both sides of the ball. Not a big leaper but controls horizontal space. Has an efficient and smooth release on his jumper. Thrives on midrange pull-ups. Has a quick stop jumper, runners and floaters. Shoots well from deep off the catch. Navigates ball screen play with expertise. Extremely tough to defend if gets going down hill. Not a complex ball handler but handles with strength. Can attack going either direction. Also a good passer when gets in the midrange area. Loves to defend and is a tough on-ball defender. Has potential to b a scrappy point guard defender.

Knight is a terrific shooter with a strong and athletic frame. His long range jumper is feathery and is utilized as a consistent weapon to stretch out a defense. Outside of his ability to drill the three ball, Knight is a bouncy athlete around the basket. He plays above the rim off two feet whenever given the chance and never hesitates to make the rim pop. A lethal threat from deep, Knight is one of the better three-point shooters in the south region.

Williams has been one of the biggest stock risers in the summer of 2021. Playing as hard as he possibly can each time he laces them up is the standard for him. He simply has one of the highest motors in all of high school basketball. Couple that with his strength, toughness, and elite athleticism and you have a high volume rebounder with a matching activity level. Williams has the physical toughness to be an excellent rebounder and defender at the high major level, however, his overall upside is in his ability to pass, dribble, and shoot. He is capable of doing all but if he will evolve in those areas once he learns how to play at more than one speed and understands pace. The bottom line is Williams' length, body type, and crazy motor will make him a productive glue guy that complements other great players around him. However, continued attention to detail on his perimeter skillset could change his trajectory as a player and would ultimately pay off in the long run.

Keller is a hard-playing power forward prospect who gets off the floor well and shows a solid foundation of skill within his game. Offensively, he diversifies his game with his physical activity on the offensive glass to score off of clean-up buckets while also having the touch to step out and knock down the long ball. He adds value as a vertical athlete who will explode off the floor to protect the rim in an intriguing fashion or to corral a rebound on both ends. The question mark within his game is his durability, however, when healthy, Keller plays with a high motor and does not require a certain amount of touches to have an effect on a game.

Brenen Lorient is a 6-foot-9, 215-pound forward who flashed serious potential during his junior season at North Texas and projects to be a major pillar for Ross Hodge's first West Virginia club. Lorient is an explosive, two-way force that projects well into any roster-construction blueprint. Lorient developed into a mismatch-hunter, capable of posting up smaller defenders and effortlessly elevating for midrange jumpers. But he's at his best playing off the ball as a fluid cutter who can use his athleticism to attack long closeouts and get downhill to finish with a vengeance. He shot over 65% at the rim and lived at the free throw line. Lorient is a low-volume 3-point shooter, but there's internal belief he can continue to elevate that part of his game. Maybe most importantly, Lorient looks every bit the part of a plus high-major defender who can offer secondary rim protection and snuff out driving lanes. The Big 12 is loaded with talented 4s. Lorient can create a ton of value if he can be West Virginia's 4-man stopper.

Despite being rated as a four-star prospect in high school, Pharrel Payne was a bit of an unknown nationally when he signed with home state Minnesota. He led the Big Ten in field goal percentage as a freshman and has never finished lower than 7th in a conference during his three years in school. He is athletic, he runs the floor and he is a capable shot blocker. He's among the most efficient big men in the country and ranked No. 5 overall in the SEC during his junior season at Texas A&M. All of the tools are in place to take a major leap as a senior at Maryland.

Reed Bailey is a bit of a tricky evaluation. He was the second-leading scorer in the A-10 and took his game to another level by leaning on his drives and nifty finishes around the rim. He's a smart cutter and is nearly ambidextrous in the paint. Bailey is a competent low-volume shooter –– which he will need to tap into to maximize his value at the high-major ranks –– but he cannot be the back-line anchor defensively. There's very little true rim protection. Opponents shot over 60% at the rim against Davidson when Bailey was on the floor.

Has a great frame at 6-10 on which to put on weight. Has a feathery and fluid athleticism that could really maximize with more physical mass. Not nesessarily a high riser or vertical athlete but is an exceptional horizontal athlete. Has a lot of wiggle to his game and can create off the dribble. Shoots it well from the outside as a vintage skilled stretch four man. Has a natural fade on his shot which creates natural space especially when shooting off the dribble. Likes to take the dribble left and come back with a right hand hook. Also has the pull back jumper coming back to his left. Can score with his back to the basket but needs to become more physical. Has surprising comfort with the ball at his size. Defense is a question at this point because of motor and strength. The potential is there to be a quality versatile defender. Can really rebound the ball when the mindset is there.

Elijah Saunders is a thick, physical forward who can step out and drain 3s and also bang in the low post for buckets. Saunders was hovering near 40% from downtown until an 0-for-10 showing in his last two games. He's a useful defender who plays hard and should be an asset in a high-major rotation.

Danny Wolf is a highly skilled big man who helped lead Yale back to the NCAA Tournament in his sophomore year while averaging 14.1 points, 9.7 rebounds, 2.4 assists, 1.3 blocks, and 1.0 steals, all while shooting 47% from the floor, 35% from three, and 72% from the free-throw line. While his height may be slightly embellished, he still possesses a rare combination of size and skill that has led to significant high-major interest out of the portal. Wolf may even have more shooting potential than we've seen so far, with naturally soft touch and clean mechanics that should yield a higher percentage when he's not the focal point of opposing defenses and utilized as a floor-spacer more frequently. He's a pick-and-pop threat and also very adept at putting the ball on the floor, giving him potential to operate out of various types of offensive actions. Athleticism and durability are key variables moving forward. He was fully healthy this year after playing 21 games as a freshman and battling various injuries in his last year of high school. He still has another level he can get to with his conditioning, that could help him on the defensive end of the floor, as well as with maintain his rebounding prowess against higher levels of competition.

Kylan Boswell is a former five-star prospect who was originally a member of the class of 2023, before reclassifying up to join Arizona as a young freshman in the summer of 2022. He played back-up minutes off the bench as a freshman before taking over the starting job as a sophomore, where he had very high expectations coming into the season after a strong summer with USA Basketball. Those expectations only gained momentum after a strong start to his sophomore campaign, but unfortunately it ended up by a season characterized by inconsistency. He finished the season by scoring 5 or less points in 5 of Arizona’s last 6 games. Boswell is a big-bodied lead guard who is strong and physical on both ends of the floor. He has a good instinctive feel for the game offensively with a better than 2:1 assist-to-turnover ratio, had bouts of struggling with both his confidence and his decision-making late in the game. It was a similar story with his shooting. He’s a career 38% three-point shooter, who is at his best in the middle third of the court, but got very streaky when confidence and focus became concerns. He’s typically a pull-up threat with deep range and valuable pick-and-roll playmaker who can make reads and utilize his strong frame to punish defenders for certain coverages. Physically, Boswell is strong with good body control. He can push the ball with pace and also has some craft and dexterity in the lane, but is not necessarily an explosive vertical athlete. Defensively, he is most effective as part of a connected half-court defense, where he can be physical and not pulled too far away from his help. His overall focus is also a key variable for his future success.

The Mountain West Player of the Year averaged 20.4 points, 6.4 assists, 2.3 rebounds, and 1.4 steals on 49/41/78 shooting in his junior season. Dent has extreme burst and speed with the ball in his hands. He can create tempo going north to south and was the engine that fueled the 7th fastest team in country last season. He also gets paint touches in a half-court game. He can blow right by most defenders with straight line drives, is a constant threat to turn the corner in a ball-screen, and can navigate traffic with his tight handle. While he’s not especially big or long, he still finishes at a steady 55% clip at the rim. Dent has made great strides with his shooting and while he’s selective with his attempts now, he shot 54% on unguarded catch and shoot threes last year. He’s also a very good passer, specifically connecting with his roll man off ball screens, but was somewhat turnover prone last season. Defensively, he’s quick and feisty, but can be undersized in certain matchups.

Conwell is a big-time shot-maker who posted 16.5 points per game last year at Xavier on 45/41/85 shooting after putting up a similar 16.6 on 48/41/86 a year earlier at Indiana State. In both seasons, he’s taken about two-thirds of his shots from behind the arc. The southpaw has a quick release that starts with getting his feet down in a hurry. He’s a legit movement shooter who can come off all types of screens. He’s got extreme gravity spacing the floor, is also pretty adept at attacking close-outs, and just a deadly shooting off the catch as he is the dribble. Conwell can be a bit turnover prone at times, but seemed more comfortable as a secondary handler and decision-maker last year. He has solid positional size at 6-foot-4, is physically strong, and competitive on both ends of the floor.

A little on the small side for a shooting guard at a thin 6-3. Is a decent athlete. Not explosive but has body control and good feet. A top notch shooter. Has a compact, smooth and quick shooting release. Shoots well cutting off screens. Off the dribble is more of a pull up guy than get to the rim guy. Doesn't yet have the strength and quickness with the ball to be a rim attacker but does a great job of creating space for his jumper. Is a solid secondary ball handler and distributor. Has a feel for scoring and for defending. A good off the ball defender. Still needs to build up strength to improve as an on the ball defender and a rebounder.

UCF was Hall’s third school in 3 years last year, but he’s made notable strides at each stop, and led the Big 12 in scoring last year. The 6-foot-7, 235-pounder is a multi-dimensional, if not always efficient, stat stuffer who averaged 18.8 points, 7.1 rebounds, 2.4 assists, and 1.3 stocks at UCF. Somewhere between a jumbo southpaw wing in the making and a mismatch four-man, Hall can score the ball in a variety of ways. He was one of the most effective drivers at the high-major level last year, averaging 0.96 points per possession. He’s a fluid handler for his size, with some definite rhythm to his game off the dribble, and yet still a willingness to be physical and play bully ball when needed. His 6.7 fouls drawn per 40 minutes was second in the Big 12, while he knocked down 81% of his free-throws. He also can’t be left unchecked beyond the arc, making 35% of his total attempts and 15 of his 29 open catch-and-shoot threes. He’s even a threat in the mid-range area, shooting 46% on two-pointers off the dribble. Yet still, there’s plenty of untapped upside as he cleans up his finishing (he shot below 50% at the rim) and decision-making (his 3 turnovers per game exceeded his 2.4 assists). Hall played with a ton of freedom and offensive volume last year, so the hope is that somewhat reduced usage can help lead to better efficiency while maintaining the same versatility. Defensively, he has the size, length, strength, and movement skills to be effective, and yet has never been part of an especially good defense before.

Has solid to good strength and solid length for a shooting guard with playmaking ability. A good athlete but not an overwhelming athlete. Has the strength, body control, and elusiveness to make plays in traffic. Adequate shooter from behind the arc but prefers to attack the rim. Prefers going to his right. Doesn't have the tightest handle but gets to his spots on the court. Is a willing passer who sees the court. Aggressive and savvy rebounder. A threat on the offensive boards. Has great potential as a three position defender.

After two seasons at Belmont, Jakobi Gillespie established himself as one of the best point guards in the country last season at Maryland, averaging 14.7 points, 4.8 assists, 2.8 rebounds, and 1.9 steals against 1.9 turnovers while shooting 45% from the field, 41% from the three-point line, and 86% from the free-throw line. The concern going into last season was that Gillespie’s lack of elite size and athleticism would make it difficult to adjust to a higher level of competition, but that was not the case. Instead, he differentiated himself by proving he could impact the game in a multitude of different ways. His shot-making translated across the board. He has a very fluid release and knocked down 46% of his open catch-and-shoot threes, both spotting up and being a real movement shooter off screens. He was also a tough shot-maker off the dribble, knocking down 38% of those threes, while showing a real ability to separate with step-backs. Gillespie was crafty and poised in the lane, compensating for whatever physicality he may lack with creativity and skill to create for himself and others. He ended up being one of just four high-major players in the country to make at least 80 threes and post an assist rate above 27.0, and had an instinct for finding the balance between scoring and distributing. Defensively, he’s competitive and smart, not only producing a high steal rate but being effective at the point of attack.

Rated as a three-star wing coming out of high school, Jaden Henley has been on the move during each year of his career. He began at Minnesota before moving on to DePaul as a sophomore and then UNLV as a junior. Even though he hasn't stayed put in one place for very long, he has managed to improve each step of the way. At 6-foot-7 he has excellent size and has turned himself into a dangerous three-point shooter. Late in his junior season at UNLV he was tasked with additional playmaking duties and played the best basketball of his career.

Williams has evolved from an undersized four-man in high school to a versatile and efficient big wing of sorts who can hunt mismatches from different spots on the floor. At 6-foot-6 with a still thicker natural build, he compensates for what he may lack in quickness or explosiveness with strength, intellect, and skill. He can post-up, put the ball on the floor in both directions, pick-and-pop, and play out of all types of offensive actions. His ability to read the game is very advanced. He averaged 3.6 assists per game, good enough for a 23% assist rate. He was not only the player that Grant McCasland often relied on most in late game situations but also allowed Texas Tech to invert a lot of their offensive actions and let him operate like a big initiator out of ball-screens and dribble hand-offs. While Williams’ game has made notable strides in each of his three seasons, beginning with his freshman year at Nevada, the one area which saw regression recently was with his three-point shooting. Williams shot just 34% from behind the arc last season, after knocking down 46% as a sophomore at Texas Tech. He’s still a plenty willing floor-spacer, and capable of making big shots in key moments, but somewhat unorthodox with his release and rotation. Defensively, his rebounding numbers also dipped, but that could be by virtue of spending more time alongside two true bigs. He’s improved his mobility enough to be reliable guarding opposing wings, so long as he isn’t overextended and has support in gaps around him. Overall, while his physical tools may not be flashy, he’s viewed as a reliable and heady team defender.

A 7-footer with a 7-foot-5 wingspan, he not only has game-changing size and length, but also terrific defensive instincts. He was one of the most dominant shot-blockers in all of prep school basketball last year, after arriving at Putnam Science Academy for the second semester. While his defense is currently ahead of his offense, he has a soft natural touch to develop and is also both a lob threat and a rim-runner who changes ends particularly well for a player his size.

A small forward out of Washington, Stevens is a long and slender wing forward who provides some versatility. He is excellent in transition where he can get out and finish above the rim. He moves well laterally, he shows promise as a mid range jump shooter and should be ale to extend his range with repetitions. He does need to add more strength, but with that added strength he should also be able to play some as a small ball four man on the next level.

Veesaar is a skilled 7-footer from Estonia who spent three years at Arizona, but missed the entire 2023-24 season with an elbow injury. After adding 35 pounds of muscle to his frame, he was able to step in and produce extremely productive minutes when Motiejus Krivas went down this season. He finished second in the Big 12 in both two-point field goal percentage (70%) and offensive rating (133.7). He was also hugely impactful as an offensive rebounder and rim protector. The increased physicality in his game allowed him to put much more pressure on the rim as both a roller and rim-runner, sometimes even as a lob threat. He has soft hands, can quickly finish with both, and has polished footwork to match. In addition to being a heady screener, he’s a good passer and can play out of high-low action or dribble hand-offs. Veesaar has a developing face-up touch and knocked down 33% of his threes this year, averaging over an attempt per game. Defensively, he may not be super bouncy, but he’s become a much more emphatic shot-blocker who now aggressively challenges shots at the rim, but could still do a better job as a defensive rebounder.

Jackson has run hot and cold as a shooter, but he's one of the best drive-first guards in this class. When Jackson wants to get to the rim, he does just that. That ability to generate paint touches should absolutely translate from the Horizon into the Big East. His first step is legit, and he plays off two feet very well. Jackson also doubles as a sturdy defender who can offer some secondary creation. Surround him with shooters, and Jackson could blossom.

Malik Moore is a smooth operator who shot over 40% from beyond the arc on 4.9 attempts per game. Moore's handle has rapidly improved, and the 6-foot-5 guard can get to his pull-up whenever he wants. He's mostly a two-level scorer who doesn't put a ton of pressure on the rim, but that jumper is buttery.

Michael Rataj is a 6-foot-9 forward who can dribble, pass and shoot, which every team needs and very few have. Rataj broke out as a junior and had nights where he looked completely unstoppable. Rataj torched Gonzaga for 29 points, seven rebounds and two dimes in mid-January using a flurry of 3s, drives and tough finishes. Rataj looks positioned to be a huge addition for a Baylor frontcourt that lost Norchad Omier.

Lee is a 6-foot-4 lead guard who plays with terrific pace and anticipation in pick-and-rolls. He's one of the most potent off-the-dribble shot-makers in the sport. His stepback jumpers are automatic, and his smooth handle and acceleration helps him shed defenders and get into the paint at will to set the table for lobs, throwbacks or his own floaters. Lee was not afraid to put his face in the fan on the glass, and he notched multiple triple-doubles for Princeton.

Treysen Eaglestaff is a rangy, 6-foot-6 wing who may have had to take the toughest shots in basketball last year. He was constantly forced to bail out North Dakota's offense. When he's cooking, Eaglestaff can put up bonkers stat lines, like a 40-burger against Alabama.

Rashad King has some pretty fascinating wraparound dimes on his resume. That secondary playmaking at 6-foot-6 and 205 pounds offers some real appeal to his transfer-up case. King also shot over 35% on catch-and-shoot 3-pointers, and he was regarded as one of the top defenders in the CAA.

Christoph Tilly is a skilled 7-footer who can stretch the floor, protect the rim and operate as a sharp pick-and-roll partner. Tilly was utilized in the dribble handoff game early and often. He caught defenders napping with fluid fake handoffs and drives to the cup. Oh, and the big fella shot over 77% at the free throw line.

Bronny James is a strong-bodied combo-guard with a well-rounded game. As the eldest son of LeBron James, his every move has been under a magnifying glass since before he played his first high school game. To his credit, that has never stopped him from playing the right way. In fact, Bronny’s best attribute may be his understanding of how to impact the game without dominating the ball or forcing bad shots. He’s unselfish, a more than willing passer, and engaged defender. The best part of his individual offense is his ability to shoot the ball from long-range. He has good mechanics and consistently knocks down open rhythm threes. As a playmaker, we still haven’t seen him develop into much of a half-court creator, but he’s capable of being explosive in transition. He can get downhill with the ball, has the strong frame to absorb contact, and is a good vertical athlete at the rim with mid-air body control to match. He can play both with and without the basketball, but is more naturally suited off the ball at this point. Overall, Bronny may not always be the spectacular talent that some assume from the namesake of the NBA’s all-time leading scorer, but he is a well-rounded two-way player with a mature floor-game.
