Burries is a big-bodied scoring guard and three-level threat. A bit older for his grade, he turned 19 in September, and is physically mature with an already powerful frame to complement his versatile attack. He was a downhill playmaker at an early age, but has developed some real feel and craft as a creator. He lacks the dynamic first-step to rely on blowing right by his defender, and can almost have a slow-mo style with physicality and balance through contact. He can elongate his strides at the end of his drives, has a terrific left hand as both a driver and a finisher, good body control, and can rise-up to hammer big dunks on unsuspecting defenders, even if he worked his way to the rim more gradually. Burries also has a very formidable mid-range game. He can dance with his handle to create space for his pull-up, and can also go to a step-back with range out beyond the arc. His shooting numbers from beyond the arc have been streaky this year – 30% in EYBL play (1.4-4.8/game) and 5-28 in three games at HoopHall West – but his release is projectable, even if not totally pure. He has more of a set-shot, with limited elevation, but soft touch. He’s proven he can come off screens and make them on the move, and is always around the rim (i.e. he very rarely has bad misses). He’s also a high percentage free-throw shooter (83% in the EYBL), who utilizes his versatility and physicality to get there in high-volume. While he is focused primarily on scoring, Burries possesses soft hands, solid floor vision, and a reliable enough handle to have some on/off ball versatility. He is also an excellent rebounding guard. Defensively, he is fully engaged, physical, and competitive, if not ultra-quick. Overall, Burries is a strong and physical scoring guard, who can get his buckets in a variety of ways, but also not one dimensional because of his ability to take reps at the point guard spot, his contributions on the defensive end, the glass, and with the competitiveness of his overall approach.

Greer is a wing who combines solid size, vertical athleticism, shooting potential, a very solid natural feel for the game, and mature approach. His basketball IQ may actually be his best attribute. He just really knows how to play. He's a good passer and quick decision-maker who often immediately knows where the ball is supposed to go. He can feed the post and pass with both hands. He cuts and moves without the ball extremely well and will run lanes hard to get easy baskets in transition. He's also a good athlete who can be a bouncy finisher, and simultaneously a lay-up maker around the rim. He's not especially long, but he has good wing size at 6-foot-6 and continues to add muscle to his solid frame. While he only made 23% of his threes during the recent EYBL season, he projects as a more reliable shooter than that when he has room and rhythm. He understands how to space the floor, has a compact release, and gravity around the arc. Greer is not a particularly dynamic ball-handler, and typically only plays with minimal bounces, but he's capable of attacking close-outs or straight-line driving with either hand. Greer has shown flashes of flying at the glass for athletic rebounds (4.4 per game in the EYBL) and also has a good acumen for the game on the defensive end of the floor, but potentially more versatility up the line-up than down. More Evaluations Evaluated 09/23/2023 Brandon Jenkins Recruiting Analyst Read

Haralson is a former McDonald's All-American who fancied himself as a jumbo initiator, but was pragmatically more of a playmaking big wing in high school. His shooting was a major liability, but his positional size, playmaking ability, and defensive tools were all assets. That all played out as expected this year at Notre Dame. He averaged 17.4 points (9th in the ACC), 4 rebounds, and 2.5 assists while shooting 53.6% from the floor (5th in the ACC), 67% from the free-throw line, and 20% from three. He was at his best putting pressure on the rim, and not surprisingly, most efficient when he had the opportunity to do so in transition or coming off pick-and-rolls (frequently on the second side of the floor). The shooting though was glaringly problematic, both in terms of his visible mechanics, and analytically. He ranked in just the 9th percentile as a catch-and-shoot threat and was a similarly minimal threat shooting off the dribble, both from 3 and 2. His passing was also a relative disappointment, as his turnovers exceeded his assists. Obviously, any shooting gains would be critical to his upside, not just for floor spacing purposes, but if defenders have to close-out on him, it opens up more of his driving game. The defensive end of the floor is another area where he could really separate himself, particularly at Tennessee, where it will be a requisite under Rick Barnes. He isn't likely to have the same usage for the Vols, but could evolve into a more efficient two-way impact player.

Harwell is gradually returning to form after missing over a year with an ACL injury. He was one of the first players to distinguish himself nationally in the class of 2025 and looked to be on the verge of another jump last fall before suffering the injury. At nearly 6-foot-6 with a recently measured 6-foot-9-plus wingspan, he has good backcourt size, strength, length, and physicality that allows him to impact the game on both ends of the floor as either a big secondary guard or more conventional wing. Offensively, his mid-range pull-up was his most defined early weapon. He had good lift into his release, sometimes even a little fade to help get it off, and used the glass well. He could also incorporate some runners into an advanced mid-range game. Now, he's extended the ease of that release out to the three-point line, shooting with range, and starting to go into the mid-post to utilize his ability to score over top of smaller defenders in different types of actions. Harwell wasn't a natural creator or dynamic handler before the injury, and his ability to pressure the rim can still be limited in a halfcourt game, even when bigger defenders switch onto him on the perimeter. However, he has always been efficient with his bounces, a solid cutter, and had the type of floor game that could fit right into offensive structure, especially when he's moving the ball freely. There were signs of real physical progression last fall too. His power was becoming a differentiator on the perimeter, he was getting loose in transition more often, and rising up to finish with more explosiveness than we had previously seen. As that vertical pop continues to return to his legs his resurgence will only continue. Defensively, he’s had stretches over the years where he’s looked tough and competitive on that end of the floor. His size and physicality are both assets that should enable him to be switchable up the line-up, and he’s also capable of being a good perimeter rebounder. These are all areas that are bound to be required, and thus more obvious strengths, next year at Houston.

O'Neal is a versatile, two-way wing who impacts winning on both ends of the floor and still has his best basketball in front of him. He's long, athletic, can make some big plays in the open floor, and is also a lob threat. He takes pride in the defensive end of the floor, utilizes his length, and is impactful on that end of the floor. He's continued to develop his half-court skill-set, become a capable rhythm shooter off the catch and simultaneously able to pull-up over top of contesting defenders in the mid-range area. Physically, he's strong without being maxed out, continuing to grow, and yet willing to throw his body around when necessary. Because he's the son of a former NBA All-Star, many people falsely assume that he's spent his entire life playing the game. That isn't necessarily true though and because he was late to the game and has such versatile tools, he has a chance to keep improving for the foreseeable future.

Toombs is a physical presence in the lane and a high-volume rebounder. He has terrific length with a 7-foot-3-inch wingspan, a better than 9-foot standing reach, and a naturally strong frame. He's improved his conditioning over the years and should be able to add significant muscle mass inside a college weight room. He was also one of the best rebounders in the EYBL last year, averaging almost 8.9 boards per game, and showing a real niche for rebounding in traffic. What differentiates Toombs from most other true centers, is that he also has an impressive acumen for the game. He's a good passer who averaged 2.1 assists per game, versus just 1.4 turnovers. He can play in the post, out of dribble hand-offs, or in ball-screen action. He's definitely the type of big man you can run offense with and has good instincts playing out of the pick-and-roll. He even shows some early ability to play out of short-rolls, both making quick reads as a passer and with the soft touch to make short mid-range shots. The rest of his individual offense isn't overly polished, but Toombs is capable inside with his back to the basket because he's physical, has good footwork, and a capable baby hook over his left shoulder. He's shown some potential to extend his touch away from the basket at times, but it's far from reliable with a low pushed release, and his overall attack has become more efficient as he's embraced more of an inside-out style. Toombs' hands seem above average, but not totally elite. His mobility has improved with his conditioning, and he's capable of running the floor with long strides when he commits to it, but has less bounce around the rim and is less instinctive in the dunker sport or as a finisher. Defensively, he bangs in the post without giving up any ground, is smart with his hands (1.1 steals & 1.0 blocks), has good instincts in drop coverage, and gradually getting more capable when pulled from the paint, but not totally fluid. Overall, what makes Toombs one of the better bigs in the class is his overlap of size, physicality, rebounding, and ability to play within an offensive structure. If he's able to further develop his athleticism, defensive versatility, or face-up offensive game, it would only increase his long-term upside. Grace is a skilled scoring guard who puts up big numbers. His jumper is his first offensive weapon, and although he has very little lift into an unorthodox release (with his weight primarily on his left leg), he's an efficient shot-maker even in high-volume (49% FG, 37% 3pt, 81% FT). He's far from one-dimensional though, and actually become much more dangerous off the bounce. Grace picks his spots well, but has a sneaky quick first step and increasing ability to get downhill with physical, straight-line drives. He attacks bad close-outs while being very efficient with his bounces, very rarely wasting any dribbles. He'll flash some occasional passing ability off the dribble and is also starting to get some reps coming off ball screens, where he uses his body well. Grace is still more of a traditional two-guard than true combo, and would do well to develop some reliable mid-range weapons, but cuts well off the ball and is capable of making different reads within various offensive actions. He's typically disciplined looking for his individual offense within the flow of the game, but has moments where he can hunt his looks. Grace may not be a naturally dynamic athlete, but he has good positional size, broad shoulders, and is making positive strides with his body. He's gotten noticeably quicker in the last year or so, more fluid athletically in the open floor, and has reportedly continued to grow though the year. He runs hard in transition and steals easy baskets when he sees an opportunity to leak out. It's a similar story defensively for Grace where he isn't the most naturally quick or fluid lateral mover, and may not have great flexibility in naturally wider hips, but he guards deceptively well. He's fully engaged, proactive in getting around screens, smart with his positioning, relatively quick with his hands, and works to stay in plays even when he gets disadvantaged. The bottom line is that while Grace doesn't have glaring physical upside, he does have undeniable sheer productivity as a skilled scoring guard with increasing versatility. He continues to improve both his overall game and his body.

Kirouac is a thin-framed big man who has a misleading slight frame but a great amount of potential. While most prospects with his size and build are uncomfortable playing through contact, Kirouac invites it when looking to score inside. He never hesitates to put his shoulder in a defender and his effort entails he wants to always be counted upon when he receives the ball and looks to score down low. He shows solid touch around the basket and his year-to-year progression shows that he has been a steadfast worker who is dedicated to getting better. Kirouac is tough but his body must develop for him to find success at the high major level. He does not mind battling but he lacks the strength needed to consistently stay on balance when operating through physicality. Given his size and length, there is no denying his upside. When adding in his effort, Kirouac shows a lot of promise. Both his body and skill level will evolve and he will have a great chance at developing into a notable big man in the ACC. Kirouac will play a more competitive schedule at Wolfeboro (N.H.) Brewster Academy during his senior campaign. Once he returns home to begin his college career, Stoudamire will have the perfect canvas to sculpt in the frontcourt.

Brown first made a name for himself as a diminutive, but extremely skilled, young guard. In recent years though, he's sprouted up to 6-foot-3 and simultaneously embraced being a true point guard, taking his game to new levels. He remains exceptionally skilled. In fact, there isn't much he can't do with the ball in his hands. He has total control of his handle, as both a creator and distributor, and a terrific left hand. He's a talented passer with excellent floor vision and capable of hitting all corners of the court. He can throw pinpoint crosscourt skip passes with both hands, zip in pocket passes off the dribble, and make all the other associated pick-and-roll reads with good timing. He can rise-up for pull-ups off the dribble and posted 45/40/91 shooting splits in 3SSB action. There are still moments when he can be streaky as a shooter, as he continues to adjust to his evolving body mechanics, but he has a very clean release and projects as a real shot-maker long-term. Brown also has very good natural pace and appears to possess a real intellect for the game as well, picking up and implementing concepts quickly. We've also seen more consistent two-way engagement as of late, with a matured motor and overall approach. The biggest limitation in his game right now is just a lack of strength. While he's grown several inches in recent years, and has a 6-foot-6 wingspan, he has a more naturally narrow frame. He can get pushed off his spots at times, on both ends of the floor. Long-term, the question is how much muscle mass his frame will be able to support and how does that impact his ability to put pressure on the rim. Depending on the setting, there are also still times when he can be ball dominant and settle for unnecessarily tough shots that can impact his overall inefficiency and cause the offense to get stagnant. Overall though, the totality of Brown's skill, his ability to read the floor, and his newfound positional size makes him as good of a true point guard prospect as there is in this class.

Young a strong lefty guard who competes on both ends of the floor. He has a nice handle on the ball and is at his best when he creates opportunities for himself by penetrating gaps and getting into the teeth of the defense. Defensively, is a terrific on-ball defender when engaged and has the potential to be relied upon to guard an opposing team's best offensive performer. Efficiency and his consistency shooting the basketball has been and will be Young's greatest challenge moving forward. He has a tendency to force offense at times which leads to him settling for tough contested shots. Additionally, he needs to make strides with his accuracy from the perimeter. Improving his ability to read the game should increase his production offensively and cause him to a reliable two-way threat in the Cowboys rotation down the road.

Tillery is a bulldog point guard. He’s undersized, but compact and powerful, and absolutely loaded with intangibles. He’s ultra-competitive, tough, and shows true leadership abilities that translate to both ends of the floor. Perhaps more than anything else, Tillery sets the tone defensively. He can pick-up for the length of the floor, pressure the ball (1.7 steals per game), get over top of screens, and is an ideal first defender at the point of attack. He’s rarely limited by his size or length because of his strength, physicality, and just the amount of sheer fight he shows. He’s also a good rebounding guard who is plenty willing to stick his nose into traffic. Tillery has great end-to-end speed and is capable of creating tempo on demand. He understands how to change speeds though and is a very good passer. He’s a bit high-volume with the ball in his hands, but he sees the floor, distributes, and is a very reliable decision-maker (6.9 assists to 2.9 turnovers). He has a good understanding of how to play out of ball screens at this stage, uses his body well around the paint, and has some dexterity as a finisher. He’s plenty athletic, with power and burst alike, but still often under-the-rim in traffic. Tillery has made strides with his shooting, but is more comfortable and accurate shooting off the dribble (86th percentile in EYBL play per Synergy Sports) than the catch (42nd percentile). Most of all, Tillery is a winner, who has played a leading role on teams that have consistently exceeded expectations, both during the prep and EYBL seasons.

A 6-foot-9 jumbo wing with a wingspan exceeding 7-feet, an emerging 200-plus-pound frame and an advanced understanding of how to get to his spots and create his own shot in a half-court game, AJ Dybantsa is the best prospect in high school basketball. The bread-and-butter of his individual offense has always been his mid-range pull-up. From an early age he was able to rise-up into a high release with good rotation on his ball. Over the years, that trademark of his arsenal has only become more dangerous as he’s developed the handle and footwork to consistently create space. Simultaneously though, he’s expanded his offensive game, begun to utilize his length and athleticism to put more pressure on the rim, and extended the consistency of his shooting range out to the arc. He’s, of course, also capable of getting loose for some extremely explosive plays in the open floor. The next step in his offensive evolution is the ability to play through more contact at the rim. When Dybantsa is at his best, he is impacting the game on both ends of the floor. His movement skills are exemplary for a player his size and he’s able to cover the court like almost no one else in high school basketball. He’s shown flashes of being able to create a lot of havoc on that end of the floor, but hasn’t yet committed to it quite as consistently as he has his scoring prowess. Dybantsa has been a celebrated prospect since before he entered high school, and has avoided many of the pitfalls of early stardom by consistently improving his game at each step in the process. Still though, he’s become accustomed to playing to the camera and can go through spurts when it becomes more of a show than a game. The time is soon coming when the level of competition won’t again allow that. He won’t be able to worry about cameras, officiating, or other distractions, and will have to make winning his singular emphasis. The bottom line is this: Dybantsa controls his own future. There is no one in high school basketball with more potential than him and if he continues to raise the bar with each passing year he has an exceptional future in front of him.

Yessoufou is a physical specimen. He’s long, strong, and athletic with a chiseled frame. He’s also a relentless competitor who plays with a constantly high motor. He’s one of the most consistently productive players in the class, not just because his physical tools can be overwhelming, but because he’s continued to develop his skill-set, and diversify his attack. Initially, Yessoufou did his damage from the mid-range and in. He made his living in the mid-post, bullying defenders with straight line-drives, getting to short pull-ups, and with his power and bounce as a finisher. More recently, we’ve seen his game extend away from the basket. He’s more capable of making open rhythm threes and ranked in the 85th percentile in shooting off the dribble during EYBL play. He has developed good footwork, learned how to initiate contact to create space for short pull-ups, and utilized a quick second bounce at the rim. Still though, there is plenty of room for future development. His handle remains a work in progress and his overall touch sometimes a bit harder. While his shooting percentages are very high (50% FG & 80% FT), he will need to learn to get to his spots more efficiently. He wants to score on every touch and can have a tendency to stop the ball from moving and force tough shots at times. Defensively, his length, strength, and motor are all major assets, even if he may be a bit tight in his hips. He has the versatility to defend multiple positions, being especially switchable up the line-up, is very verbal, and also a good wing rebounder.

Williams-Adams is evolving into a highly versatile four-man who specializes in getting downhill as a face-up driver and then guarding multiple positions on the defensive end of the floor. The missing link right now is a reliable jumper to space the floor consistently. Williams-Adams is extremely long with a nearly 7-foot-2 wingspan and a solid early build that has filled out and blossomed into a strong body. He's pretty light on his feet when running the floor and a plus athlete in transition. Offensively, he plays primarily on the perimeter but is looking to put the ball on the floor and get downhill at every opportunity. He's a bit upright, but has good burst out of his legs. He's good off a pitch or when he has room to just accelerate into space. He's able to drive both sides, can navigate the lane with euro-steps, and is even capable of making some basic drive-and-kick passes after a couple of hard dribbles. He can be a bit turnover prone when trying to do too much or over-thinking. Williams-Adams showed some shooting potential when he was younger, but just hasn't progressed in that area. He made just 3 of his 20 attempted threes during the 2024 EYBL season and seems more reluctant to shoot now than when he was younger. His defensive versatility is potentially his best asset. Williams-Adams is extremely switchable on that end of the floor and legitimately able to slide his feet and keep opposing guards in front of him. He'll show flashes of getting rebounds in traffic and is bouncy enough with multiple jumps to tip balls to himself with both hands. While he has fairly modest rebounding totals (4.1 per game), at least part of that is because his defensive versatility pulls him away from the paint so frequently. Overall, Williams-Adams has evolved into more of a four-man than the potential big wing some projected when he was younger, but his versatility is his calling card, especially on the defensive end of the floor. He's long, athletic, gets downhill, and if the shooting ever catches up, it will unlock a new level of potential.

Kai Rogers was one of the most productive big men in the UAA this year, averaging 16 points on 66% shooting from the floor, nearly seven rebounds, and over two blocks. He is a true post with good size, extremely long arms (7-foot-4 wingspan), and a big body. He plays primarily with his back to the basket, but has the soft hands and touch to eventually diversify his attack. What he's best at right now is utilizing his size to attack smaller defenders. He does a good job of working for deep post position prior to the catch, understands high-low angles, and then goes toward the rim once he gets the ball. He'll look to back his defender down off the dribble if he catches in the mid-post and then likes to spin or drop-step, showing good footwork both with and without the ball. Rogers is somewhat ground bound in traffic, but can make lay-ups with both hands around the rim, and has some very sneaky bounce when he's able to elevate in space. He shows flashes of face-up touch and even some glimpses of passing potential, but isn't yet ready to make those consistent staples of his game yet (he shot 55% from the free-throw line and averaged just 0.7 assists against 2.2 turnovers per game). Rogers has gotten noticeably stronger over the years, but needs to continue to prioritize his fitness and commit to playing with a consistently high motor. While he doesn't shy away from contact in the lane, he's not always as forceful going through it as he should be and can also seem to struggle with balance in the lane at times. Similarly, he has bursts of running the floor, but can also be content to trot behind the play at times. Defensively, his big body and hands are assets, and help him to be a solid area rebounder. He's very aggressive looking to make plays on the ball, and has good metrics because of it (2.3 blocks and 0.9 steals per game), but will have to pick his spots much more judiciously at the next level. There are moments when he gets foul-prone by being slightly late to the play or out of position, and again a lot of that comes back to being balanced and proactive. Overall, Rogers was one of the best true centers in the UAA this year and has clear room for future growth with improved conditioning, development of his face-up tools, and improved balance, timing, and consistent positioning on both ends of the floor.

Thomas is one of the most naturally talented and dynamic guards in the national class. He’s extremely confident and assertive, playing with a high-volume scoring mentality. He’s a tough shot-taker and maker, with the versatility to shoot off the dribble, with range, create space for step-backs, or make runners in a crowded lane. He has a creative and polished handle to match. He can attack with pace in the open floor, be very smooth at times, has good use of both hands, and an advanced finishing package. While he goes through spurts of extreme tough shot-making, he can also be inefficient with a tendency to hunt tough shots and plays. He can settle for contested looks and also get stuck in first gear and play like he’s in a hurry at times. He’s shown some progress within the last year in terms of putting pressure on the rim and creating offense for others, instead of settling for tough twos, but can still too often be akin to a baseball player who is almost always swinging for the fences. If and when he learns to slow down and pick his spots, he’ll be able to eliminate some of the highs and lows from his game, but the key is going to be doing that without turning down his competitiveness, because he really plays hard. He’s vocal and aggressive on the defensive end of the floor, has a real knack for getting his hands on the ball, and is also an excellent rebounding guard. Thomas came up the ranks as a solid, but largely unspectacular athlete, but is also continuing to ascend physically. Standing at just under 6-foot-3 (without shoes) with a 6-foot-6 wingspan, he’s continued to get stronger and add more burst to various aspects of his game. He’s showing more speed with the ball in his hands, explosiveness at the cup, and wearing notably more muscle mass on his frame.

Evans is powerful with a muscular build, very long arms (6-foot-10 wingspan), and big hands. In addition to his college ready body, he also has a mature approach. He was an early bloomer physically, who developed a big reputation as an underclassman, in large part because he was often able to overpower the opposition. Within the last year though, his approach and floor game have shown clear and impressive maturation. He’s really embraced the defensive end of the floor. While his lateral quickness isn’t necessary his best asset, his length, strength, physicality, and competitiveness on that end make him effective. He’s capable of defending multiple positions, being especially switchable up the line-up, and is even active off the ball blowing up dribble hand-offs and being diligently disruptive. Offensively, he came up the ranks with an aggressive scoring mindset and an ability to get downhill in the open court. He’s become much more efficient within the last year though with his ability to play with and off of other talented players. He’s learned how to space the floor, make extra passes, and cut off the ball. He’s also made progress as a spot-up shooter and is starting to show some potential as a pick-and-roll threat on the second side of the floor. While he may lack some wiggle and elite explosiveness, Evans other physical tools enable him to play multiple positions too. As his guard skills continue to progress, that versatility will only expand. He also offers plenty of intangibles with his work-ethic, maturity, competitiveness, and potential leadership ability.

Johnson is a southpaw scoring guard with solid positional size at 6-foot-4, a long 6-foot-8-inch wingspan, and a still lean frame. He’s smooth, slippery, and has extremely soft natural touch in all aspects of his individual offense. He’s a shot-maker first and foremost with a high-arcing ball and great rotation. He’s capable of making some very tough shots off the catch and the dribble, including deep step-backs, and will also have a ton of gravity as a floor-spacing threat. He made 41% of his threes during his junior year at Link Academy and while that number decreased to 28% during the recent EYBL season, that was more a reflection of his shot-selection, with close to 8 attempts per game. He’s very crafty in the mid-range area, with a deep bag of runners, floaters, and wrong-footed finishes. He's capable of making some plays off the bounce and being a secondary pick-and-roll threat, but often angled off the line of his drive because of his lack of physical strength. He can similarly show some legit bounce in the open court, but rarely among bodies at this point. He can also get lackadaisical with his decision-making and motor at times, leading to some inefficient metrics (38% FG and 3.4 assists vs. 3.8 turnovers in EYBL). When Johnson gets going, he can be a microwave type scorer who can get buckets in bunches. The key for him, in addition to building up his body, is being able to impact the game when the ball isn’t in his hands or he’s not in the midst of a scoring outburst.

Magwood is one of the most naturally talented bucket-getting guards in the national class. He's a tough shot-maker who is capable of getting his shot off whenever he wants and yet still posted incredibly efficient splits of 53/52/80, while scoring almost 23 points per game, during the recent 3SSB season. He lacks great size, measuring at 6-feet even without shoes, and has a naturally leaner frame that is just starting to gradually fill-out, but compensates with very long arms and a better than 6-foot-5 wingspan. That length, plus a high release point above his head, is the basis for why he can get his pull-up and step-back off at any given time. He's just as potent a shooting threat from long range, a fluid handler, and crafty in the lane with floaters and wrong-foot finishes. He's not only a threat at all three levels, but also a spurt scorer who makes shots in bunches once he gets hot. Magwood is competitive, aggressive, and extremely confident, almost to a fault at times. He can be guilty of dominating the ball and settling for contested shots. He's a capable passer when he wants to be, but wired to score on every catch and typically only interested in moving the ball when he doesn't have his own shot. He'll inevitably have to adapt to playing with less volume at the next level, so shot selection and decision making will continue to be key variables. Defensively, he's active looking to make plays (1.7 steals per game) and shows glimpses of being able and willing to dig in and get stops, but has to be able to sustain that mentality.

Moreno is a skilled big with significant size. He measures in at just a shade under seven-feet (6’10.5” without shoes), and while he doesn’t have much additional length (7’1” wingspan), he’s coordinated and balanced for his size, with a sturdy base. He has good hands, footwork, and keeps making consistent strides with both his physical strength and athleticism. What differentiates Moreno from any other top big in the class is his ability to be efficient offensively in so many spots on the floor and thus within various offensive actions. This requires versatile skill, and a high aptitude for the game. He’s a legit low-post scoring threat who understands how to seal, has touch on his jump hook, can turn both shoulders when deep enough, and dunk with both hands. He’s a good pick-and-roll partner with the frame to set big screens, the ability to make reads and slips, and the hands to make tough catches in the lane. He’s also the type of big man you can play through at the high post, as he has good poise as cutters go through the lane, is a good passer, can play out of subsequent dribble hand-off actions, and even throw back-door passes off the dribble. Where we’re seeing Moreno work to extend his skill-set most recently is in trying to start the break himself for a couple of bounces and develop his floor-spacing potential, although he first must become a more reliable free-throw shooter (56% in 3SSB play). While he’s not naturally mobile or bouncy, because he’s continued to develop physically and athletically, we’ve seen correlated defensive growth as well. His size makes him a real deterrent in the lane and a solid rim protector when he’s already anchored in the paint (2.1 blocks). He projects as mostly a drop-coverage pick-and-roll defender, and can be flat-footed when pulled away from basket defensively, and yet has a good understanding of how to use his size to stay in plays and not give up terrible angles. Moreno was also one of the better rebounders on the 3SSB circuit this year (8.4 per game). Again, his sheer size and hands are major assets in this area, but he’s also made clear strides with his willingness to be physical inside. There are still moments where he can leave you wishing he would just grab the ball more emphatically though, and his overall assertiveness is a key long-term variable for his future success.

Blair is a 6-foot-6 southpaw and true mismatch problem because while he's built like a prototypical wing, he plays with the ball in his hands and initiates offense like a big point guard with his high school team. His best individual offense comes from the mid-range and in. He attacks both sides off the bounce, really gets the ball out in front of him in the open floor, and covers a lot of ground with each bounce. In tighter spaces, he still has a quality first-step and then utilizes his footwork well with spins, step-throughs, shot-fakes and other maneuvers at the end of his drives. He gets terrific lift on his mid-range pull-up, sometimes even hanging to shoot over top of contesting defenders, and yet still has a soft touch. He finishes with both hands, has a quick second bounce to follow his own misses, and is also a quality passer. He doesn't waste much movement in his game and can have moments where he stands and watches without the ball in his hands, and yet he's a decisive cutter when he decides to move without the ball. Blair has worked to become a more consistent shooting threat from behind the arc, but that's still his biggest offensive swing skill and not yet a reliable part of his arsenal. In one sense, that could be a limitation at the next level. Conversely though, he's undoubtedly a player who will benefit from being surrounded by more shooting, not unlike Jeremiah Fears at Oklahoma this year. Blair carries himself very confidently, and is simultaneously also competitive with leadership skills. He's willing to take on challenges defensively, but often ends up guarding frontcourt players with his high school team. Because he rarely played high-level grassroots basketball, defending elite perimeter players in the SEC is going to be an adjustment, but eventually he projects as being versatile and effective on that end of the floor. He's also a good perimeter rebounder who is very adept at taking the ball off the defensive glass to start the break himself. The two-sport star is also a plus athlete with big hands and a high IQ.

Mandaquit is a throwback true point guard who excels with his ability to run a team. He isn't going to wow anyone with dynamic explosiveness or individual offense, but makes his mark with his reliability, acumen for the game, and winning intangibles. He's at his best in USA Basketball settings, where he's surrounded by elite talent, and can be a table-setter and leader. Where he's not as naturally suited is when he has to carry too much of the offensive load or be a primary creator. At right around 6-foot-1, Mandaquit is extremely strong with a nearly 200-pound frame. He has big hands, a broad upper body, and is physically tough. He's poised and strong with the ball using jab-steps and rip-throughs to create space. His head is constantly up, he sees the game develop ahead of most others, and can zip long chest passes on a rope when he's advancing the ball. He's very reliable getting the team into their sets or actions, even against pressure, and had a 3:1 assist-to-turnover ratio in FIBA play. The most important variable in Mandaquit's individual offense is the progression of his shooting. He converted just 21% of his threes and 59% of his free-throws during 3SSB play, and was similarly just 4-19 from behind the arc in FIBA play. He has relatively clean mechanics that seem to be trending in the right direction and good rotation on his ball, but must become a willing and consistent spot-up threat in order to keep opposing defenses honest. He really works defensively and is most effective in quarter-court situations, where he can rely on his strength, competitiveness, and good hands (2 steals per game in 3SSB play), without getting too overexposed away from his help. He's also a very good perimeter rebounder. Overall, Mandaquit is a throwback floor general who compensates for some athletic limitations with his toughness, leadership, defensive tenacity, IQ for the game, and understanding of how to run a team.

Wilkins is a budding big wing with extreme athleticism and a ton of long-term potential. He runs the floor with long, bouncy strides and plays above the rim like few others in the country. He's extremely quick off his feet, able to play up at the top of the box, and also contort his body in mid-air. He can be dynamic in transition and also make the type of highlight finishes that immediately change the momentum of a game. He's an active offensive rebounder and also has significant defensive potential as well, due to his court coverage and ability to fly around the floor. Wilkins' most glaring limitation right now is a thin, and almost completely undeveloped, frame. He's lanky and narrow through his core, with a corresponding lack of length for his size (measured 6'7.5" wingspan in June of 2023), and yet an extremely elastic and flexible body type. For all his bounce, body control, and even touch at the rim, he can often struggle to finish through traffic unless he is able to rise above the pack. While he has naturally soft hands and so some potential as a handler and a passer, he's upright and unable to play through any contact there either. Defensively, there are plenty of times where he gets overpowered as well. While he will undoubtedly be a totally different player once he grows into his frame, his shooting is also a work in progress. His natural touch isn't bad and he's capable of making some open threes, but he can also really spray contested ones as he has a tendency to lean back into his release and shoot a much harder ball when he's not in complete rhythm. Overall, Wilkins remains a hugely intriguing long-term prospect because of his highest-level athleticism, explosiveness in transition, and defensive potential, but the overall productivity needs to start being more consistent, particularly in a half-court game. White is one of the most explosive athletes in the national class. He is super bouncy, able to play up at the top of the box, and quick with his second (and third) bounces. He is not only an elite lob threat, but the type of finisher who can immediately change the momentum of the game with a highlight dunk. Beyond his leaping ability, White is also a versatile athlete. He moves with pace and force, covering the court and playing through contact with increasing frequency. Physically, he has the length to match his athleticism with a nearly 7-foot wingspan and 8-foot-11 standing reach. He has broad shoulders, a sturdy base in his lower body, and continues to fill-out his wiry frame. From a skill standpoint, White is still a work in progress. He is not yet a consistent shooting threat from the perimeter and can get inefficient when trying to prove otherwise or settling for low percentage face-up shots. What the lefty does have is an advanced right hand, an expanding ability to get downhill, and play through contact. There are even some occasional signs of a passing instinct. While he’s probably at his best offensively in the open floor right now, he’s already a weapon on the defensive end of the floor. He’s physically capable of guarding multiple positions as well as being a playmaker from off the ball, and already takes pride in asserting himself on that end of the floor.

Anthony is a skilled, smooth scorer and high-volume shot-taker and maker. He plays with an aggressive scoring mentality and is constantly hunting buckets. He’s had the ultimate green-light, particularly in the EYBL, and so he has a tendency to settle for tough shots at times (38% FG, 29% 3pt in EYBL), but there are few players in high school basketball better equipped to make shots with such high degrees of difficultly. He has pull-ups, step-backs, floaters, and deep threes, all with naturally soft touch, and instinctive feel for putting the ball in the basket. His footwork and jab series are as advanced as you’d expected and he’s also become very adept at coming off of ball-screens, ranking in the 90th percentile in those situations during the 2024 EYBL per Synergy. While his scoring prowess is undoubtedly his defining characteristic at this point, he’s also continued to grow and expand his guard skills, thus trending towards more of a big guard. He’s smooth with his handle and actually a better passer than he typically gets credit for, when he’s willing. He hasn’t measured out as particularly long, but he’s continued to grow and now above 6-foot-5 with shoes on. He’s a solid, but not elite, athlete who doesn’t always create a ton of separation in a half-court game, but still has no problem getting his shot off. He’s also continued to get stronger and begun to add some important physicality to his game. Overall, Anthony already has an advanced game and a proven track record of continuing to make notable improvements with each passing season. There’s plenty of potential to continue ascending too as he gets stronger, embraces the details on the defensive end of the floor, and becomes more efficient offensively by picking his spots and improving his shot-selection without dominating the ball so frequently. When it comes to scoring the basketball, few in the class of 2025 are better than Ellis. While he's not the biggest guy around, there's absolutely no questioning his skill, toughness and creativity when it comes to finding ways to put the ball in the hoop. Ellis is a dangerous shooter from deep with nearly unlimited range and he is quite adept at creating shot attempts for himself via the dribble. He loves to get to his spots for mid range pull-ups, has an assortment of floaters at his disposal and plays with complete and total confidence. There is room for Ellis to become a bit more efficient as a scorer and he has room to develop as a playmaker for others but to be clear, we are talking about a guy who is wired to score the basketball.

Oliogu is a big-bodied wing athlete and downhill creator, who may have enough size, length, and strength to play a similar role as an undersized playmaking forward. He's extremely powerful with a thick build and also an explosive leaper who can rise up to hammer violent dunks. Oliogu is at his best in the open floor. He is able to get downhill like only a select few others when going from north to south, but also has an underrated ability to decelerate and navigate traffic with euros. Oliogu is not a bad handler and passer, and also has a good left hand, but he can be turnover-prone. His perimeter shooting remains unreliable and he becomes much less efficient when trying to prove otherwise. Oliogu typically plays with a high motor, has some defensive versatility onto bigger guys, and a playmaking instinct on that end of the floor. Another major key, along with the shooting, is Oliogu's body composition. He's almost gotten too bulky at times which has reduced his mobility and agility on both ends of the floor.

With two Florida state championships and three Nike EYB championships, Allen is one of the winningest players in all of high school basketball. A big, power guard, he’s long and versatile with the type of skill-set and basketball IQ you would expect from a coach’s son. Allen is strong and physical with a naturally broader frame, good positional size at 6-foot-3, and a 6-foot-7 wingspan. He’s multi-positional on both ends of the floor. Offensively, he’s a true combo-guard who can play both on and off the ball. He may not be loaded with natural quickness, but he can really get downhill when he has momentum and has some sneaky bounce in those situations as well. He’s a heady passer who takes very good care of the basketball (3:1 assist-to-turnover ratio in EYBL) and a good cutter off the ball. The shooting is a key long-term variable. He has a clean ball when he has room and rhythm, but can be streaky at times and not always able to generate the same consistent rotation. Allen is a very good defender, who is competitive and takes pride in impacting the game on that end of the floor. It's not ideal for him to get too over-extended away from his help, but he's physical and strong enough to be switchable up the line-up, and also a very good rebounding guard. Overall, there just aren’t too many players in the country who are more consistently productive or able to impact winning to the same extent. He’s long, powerful, physical, skilled, extremely versatile, and a true stat stuffer. Additionally, he has a wealth of intangibles because he’s smart, competitive, and verbal.

Flemings is a playmaking lead guard loaded with explosive athleticism. He owns a rare overlap of terrific speed with the ball, quick twitch burst, and a very shifty type of stop-and-go attack. He’s at his best when the game is going up and down because he’s dynamic in the open floor in a way that can look effortless at times. He plays well above the rim, often dunking the ball on his way up from his initial jump. He gets paint touches in a half-court game as well, despite playing fairly upright at times, because he can really turn the corner on a ball-screen and is a good passer off the dribble as well. He plays with a similarly aggressive playmaking mentality on the defensive end of the floor. There are moments when he could get lower to the ground in his stance, but he’s typically competitive and has terrific hands on that end of the floor that allow him to generate a lot of steals. His shooting is his biggest swing skill. He’s capable with his mid-range pull-up right now, but less reliable from three. Mechanically, he has excess motion going into his release, generating the vast majority of his power from his arms, and then a snapped release that creates some occasional sidespin. Mallory may be undersized and play under the rim, but there are few more reliable true point guards in the national class. His skill level with the ball is truly elite. He has complete command of his handle and a pure release on his jumper. He not only shoots it with deep range, but is equally comfortable off the catch or the dribble, and has the effortless quick release to get it off despite his lack of size. Mallory can stop quickly for mid-range pull-ups with deceptive lift and also has a deep bag of floaters and runners that he can utilize without breaking his stride inside a crowded lane. While he only played seven games in the UAA this season, his 45/38/90 shooting splits are indicative of what we have come to expect. Simultaneously, Mallory is also exceptionally reliable with the ball in his hands. He's extremely strong with the ball, rarely rattled by pressure, a very good passer, and a pinpoint lob thrower (4.2 assists vs. 1.7 turnovers). He's even starting to show more creativity and playmaking with the ball in his hands, all without limiting his efficiency. Mallory is also a diligent defender who can pick up the ball for the length of the floor, fight over ball screens, has good hands (1.6 steals in the 2024 UAA and 2.1 in 2023), and will dig out long rebounds. While there are likely to be some moments at the next level where his size will limit him on that end of the floor, it won't be due to a lack of fight or intellect. Mallory's value is also magnified by his wealth of intangibles and true leadership skills. He's not only a throwback true point guard, but one of the most skilled players in the class, with the ideal mental make-up to compensate for whatever he may be lacking in terms of physical measurables.

Jackson is a high-volume scoring guard who has a lot of game with the ball in his hands, but has historically been reliant on a combination of volume and freedom in order to be at his best. When he gets those two things, he has put up some very impressive numbers, particularly early in his career. More recently, we've seen him struggle to blend that ability into the team concept when he's had to share the ball with other talented guards. At 6-foot-2 with a 6-foot-6 wingspan, Jackson is a long, wiry, and somewhat lean playmaker who wants to iso or attack off ball-screens. He's more of a shot-maker than a pure shooter, and more accurate off the dribble than the catch, but he's a threat to make pull-ups from the three-point line and in. He's really tight with his handle, instinctively tries to split every trap he faces, gets into the lane consistently, and has a full assortment of floaters and runners. While he can make tough shots, it can come at the expense of his team's ball movement. But he's also capable of passing off the dribble, with both hands, and creating for others. The key for success at the next level will rely on him finding a spot where he is allowed to play with his naturally aggressive scoring mentality or for him to learn to utilize his scoring prowess better within the concept of a team's collective attack.

Jones is a strong and athletic combo-forward who plays an aggressive brand of basketball. He gets downhill with extreme force, averaging nearly five points per game in transition alone, and gets a ton of easy points by both finishing above the rim - he ranked second amongst all freshmen in the country last year with 54 dunks - and getting to the free-throw line - he was third overall in the Big East in free-throw rate. He also has the positional versatility to play as a physical, smallball four-man or a jumbo-wing, particularly if he's paired with a stretch-four. Defensively, he has all the physical tools to be switchable and positionally versatile, but can be erratic with his decision-making and understanding of coverages and rotations. His offensive decision making and shooting could also evolve. While he shot 57% from the floor and nearly 39% from three, that was while being very selective with his attempts, averaging just one per game. His turnovers exceeded his assists as he could get tunnel vision at times, instead of utilizing his full mismatch potential. Jones also doesn't approach the defensive glass with quite the same motor as he does his scoring opportunities.

Steger is a team-oriented skilled shooting guard who plays under control and makes good decisions on the floor. He is not the most athletic but is very fundamentally sound in the way he goes about his businness on the offensive end. Steger is a streaky shooter who bases his game from behind the three-point arc. He is comfortable in the catch-and-shoot game and is also a threat with his long ball in transition. He knows how to play and is bound to flourish in offensive structure at the next level. No action is forced when it comes to Steger's game. There are times where he could stand to be more aggressive but he is one who is comfortable playing off of great talent by letting the game come to him.

Sealy is a strong, versatile and athletic perimeter player who is capable of playing several roles on a team. For his high school team he has played largely at the point guard position while he's been able to move all around the perimeter during grassroots play. Where Sealy excels and is at his best is with the ball in his hands, driving to the rim. He is able to play through contact thanks to his strength and size and he can be a bouncy finisher once he gets all the way to the rim. He is also a capable playmaker for others in those driving situations, but needs to improve some on his decision making at times. He sees the floor plenty well, but can be a bit ambitious in looking to hit the home run when the simple play is more readily available. Because he likes to drive, Sealy also does an effective job of earning trips to the free throw line. He is a willing jump shooter and his shot looks pretty clean, but in a limited sample size available on Synergy, he is far more comfortable shooting off the dribble than he is as a catch and shoot guy. So, the jumper is certainly an area where he has the most room to improve.

Mosley is a late-blooming big guard who is only just beginning to fill out his frame, but possesses a very intriguing long-term combination of perimeter size, skill, and fluidity. He shoots a very good-looking ball from long-range, but only made 28% of his threes in this year's UAA season. The jumper projects as a critical part of his value at the next level, so being more consistent is very important. While he typically plays off the catch from the wing area – where he has a good sense of spacing, how to play off other people, and a complimenting pull-up game – he's also shown some signs of playmaking and passing off the dribble more recently. Mosely's physical progression is an important long-term key, both to his playmaking potential and total floor game. While he has good positional size, he lacks ideal length. He's not yet fully explosive or strong enough to consistently play through contact. As a handler, he lacks a great first step, but can be fluid with his secondary moves if he's able to get an initial angle and make some basic pick-and-roll reads. Right now, Mosely can also be overpowered at times defensively and needs to commit himself to improving on that end. Patience is going to be a long-term requisite in Mosley's development, but there are a lot of reasons to be optimistic given his perimeter size and skill level, especially if he can evolve from more of a catch-and-shoot wing player into more of a multi-dimensional big guard.

Walls is a high-upside prospect who not only has elite tools but fits a coveted big man prototype in the modern game. He's a mobile big man who can run, jump, and potentially stretch the floor both vertically and laterally while simultaneously being a versatile interior defender who can block shots. The problem is that his overall production does not always live up to that potential. Walls is coming off an EYBL season where he averaged 7.1 points per game, shot under 40% from the floor, and grabbed 4.3 rebounds per game. While he's capable of making open threes --- he made over a three per game in the EYBL while shooting 33% and 67% from the free-throw line --- he has unorthodox mechanics where he slings the release from his shoulder and his elbow flies out. He took over half his shots from long range and didn't always make a consistent impact in the lane. Physically, he's narrowly built in his core and shoulders, so not quite as long as he looks with a wingspan just over 6-foot-10. He's a very fluid mover and athletic for his size, but physically weak and not yet able to consistently play through contact. Walls is a lob threat, able to beat opposing bigs up the floor, and capable of flying at the offensive glass, albeit not always intent to do it. He'd be ideal for run and jump systems and has significant defensive upside because of his movement skills and court coverage. Overall, the upside for Walls at the next level is undeniable, but patience is definitely a requisite for him because he must build up his body and keep developing his overall offense. Simultaneously, Walls has to commit to fully utilizing his physical tools to be more consistently impactful and not be content to sit solely on the perimeter. Khamenia's feel for the game and basketball IQ was immediately apparent as a freshman at Duke. He cut well off the ball, knew how to come off screens, and was a valuable ball-mover, even if he didn't get much of a chance to show the full scope of his passing creativity. Khamenia also proved to be a valuable floor spacer, connecting on 44% of his catch-and-shoot threes, but was not a shooting threat off the dribble (he attempted one jumper off the dribble all season per Synergy), nor a tough shot-maker (20% on guarded catch-and-shoot threes). We still saw the same physical limitations that existed in high school. He's not overly quick or explosive, and he could better sculpt his frame. Some of those limitations impact him on the defensive end of the floor, but he was part of a team that ranked 3rd overall in adjusted defensive efficiency. Still, finding the right match-up is important and he's a more reliable team defender, who can be trusted to be in the right spots, than he is on the ball. It also remains to be seen if he rebounds or finishes (he only had 2 dunks all year long) well enough to have the positional versatility to play the 4, or if he has to be utilized like a jumbo wing.

Jalen Reece is a lead guard who isn’t going to wow you physically, but is extremely talented with the ball in his hands. He has a very tight handle and advanced ability to shoot off the dribble. While he lacks great size and plays almost exclusively under the rim, he has no problem getting his pull-up off thanks to a high and deep release point. He has his handle on a string and regularly puts together combination moves to create separation on demand. Reece can also make deep pull-ups, well beyond the arc, but may be a bit more comfortable shooting off the dribble than the catch. He converted 34% of his threes, along with 70% of his free-throws, during EYBL play. Simultaneously though, he showed he could create for others while taking care of the ball with 4.4 assists against 2.1 turnovers. His head is always up in the open floor and he’s very adept at zipping in quick pocket passes when playing out of ball screens. At 6-feet with a solid, but fairly modest, build, there will be questions about how his finishing and defense will translate to the next level. He’s tough, competitive, and confident on both ends though. He has a full assortment of floaters and crafty finishes in the lane and equally good hands on the defensive end of the floor (1.7 steals).

Lewis is a lead guard that wants to play fast and get out in transition. He plays with great pace, but can also be shifty changing speeds on his way to the paint. He is in constant attack mode, so he can look like a scoring guard or get himself into tough spots at times, but he’s an underrated overall passer and distributor who averaged 3.9 assists vs. 1.6 turnovers in the recent EYBL season. In the half-court, his first-step is best when attacking right off the catch. He’s generally below the rim and can struggle to finish in traffic (42% 2pt FG% in EYBL play), but can also make crafty lay-ups and tough floaters with relatively deep range. His left hand is very good, as both a passer and finisher. He’s never been a pure shooter, but was trending in the right direction before seeming to lose some confidence this summer. He ended up shooting 37% from the field and just 19% from behind the arc in EYBL play. He projects as a more capable shooter than that long-term though, and did knock down 86% of his free-throws, and seems to have his confidence back in his senior season. Physically, Lewis is a bit undersized, measuring at 6-feet even without shoes on, but has some length with a 6-foot-4 wingspan, and has added a new element of power to both his game and frame within the last year. He’s a tenacious and competitive defender who really challenges people on that end of the floor and can set the tone at the point of attack. He’s also a good rebounding guard. Overall, his most immediate impacts are going to be made with his pace in the open court and defensive toughness. If he learns when to slow down, pick his spots, and the shooting proves consistent, it unlocks higher upside outcomes.

While Cenac has emerged at a rapid rate in recent months, he is really still just scratching the surface of his potential and has as much untapped long-term upside as maybe any prospect in the national class. What is particularly intriguing about Cenac is the versatility and scope of his natural ability. He checks all the boxes from size to physical tools to budding skill. Physically, he’s 6-foot-10 with a long, 7-foot-3 wingspan, and a better than 9-foot-1 standing reach. He’s cut and defined with a 233-pound frame and yet still plenty of room to keep filling out. He doesn’t move like a traditional big man though. He’s mobile, fluid, gets off his feet with ease athletically, and even relatively elastic in terms of the way he can bend and stretch his frame within the flow of the game. Those movement and athletic tools give him a lot of budding versatility on the defensive end of the floor. He’s shown clear potential both as a rim protector and being able to move laterally away from the basket, enabling him to defend a couple of different positions and projecting as versatile in ball-screen coverages as well. He also has spurts where he can be dominant on the defensive glass. There’s similar budding versatility on the offensive end of the floor. While he can hammer big dunks inside and be a lob threat, he’s also comfortable putting the ball on the floor and shows early shooting potential out to the arc with soft natural touch and great rotation. He even has a growing passing instinct and terrific left hand that allows him to drive opposing bigs to both sides, finish and pass with dexterity. What comes next is turning that undeniable potential into consistent production against the highest levels of competition. Offensively, that means figuring out exactly where his best spots are and how to get there efficiently. Defensively, fully utilizing his mobility and shot-blocking while staying committed to dominating the glass. The bottom line is that his tools are as immense as they are versatile, but he can be plagued by inconsistent body language, assertiveness, and even motor at times. With continued growth and maturation though, his long-term upside is very high.

Scott is a big wing with versatile potential on both ends of the floor. In addition to his positional size, he's a very active wing rebounder, has good defensive metrics, a developing shooting stroke from behind the three-point line, a good feel for the game, and an understanding of how to play within the team concept. However, he's still fairly undeveloped physically, can struggle to play through contact, and doesn't always assert himself like a top prospect should be capable of. Scott is a willing passer and reliable decision-maker with the ball in his hands (2:1 assist-to-turnover ratio in UA Rise play), and likes to take smaller defenders into the mid-post where he can utilize his size to shoot over top of them, but doesn't otherwise put much pressure on the rim, showing very little creativity or playmaking as a handler at this point. The most translatable part of Scott's offensive game right now is probably his three-point shooting. He's not a totally pure shooter, but made almost 36% of his attempts on high-volume (7+ attempts per game), while showing deep range and a relatively compact release. There is similar projectability on the defensive end given his size, active hands, nose for the ball (2.6 stocks per game), and bursts of movement. Overall, Scott has size, two-way tools, and overall instincts that translate up levels, and thus clear long-term upside, but he's farther away physically than most four-star prospects and thus going to require some patience before he can build up his body to play through the level of contact he's going to see at the next level.

Dixon is the ultimate utility guard. He has good positional size, can play on or off the ball, is a reliable decision maker (2.4-to-1 as a freshman as North Carolina), shoots it well (40% 3pt on nearly 4 attempts per game and 47% on unguarded catch-and-shoot threes), and is a versatile half-court defender. What he isn't necessarily is overly dynamic. He's more skilled and efficient than he is particularly explosive or creative. He can make pick-and-roll reads, and is a particularly good lob passer, but isn't the type of iso player you can rely on in late shot clock situations. 79% of his shots were jumpers last year, and when he did get to the rim, he really struggled to finish, making just 28% of his attempts. The bottom line is that Dixon is incredibly solid, and reliable, on both ends of the floor, but his usage and role will be critical to getting the most efficient version of him.

Rencher is strong, physical, and athletic with good perimeter size at 6-foot-5 and a mature frame. He plays hard and competes on both ends of the floor. He's best described as a wing at this point, but has a growing skill-set and is trending towards being a multi-positional big guard of sorts. He's made significant strides as a spot-up shooter (41% 3pt in 3SSB). He has terrific shot preparation and good rotation, despite shooting a bit of a hard ball. He's still evolving a bit as a handler and passer. He doesn't get the ball out in front of him when pushing himself and has an easier time overpowering defenders then blowing right by them in the half-court. He likes physicality though, has good balance through contact, and a combination of bounce and power that leads to some emphatic finishes. He has good perimeter footwork, but tends to stop the ball from moving when he wants to face-up his defender and jab at him in an isolation style. He's a capable pull-up shooter, but can settle for tough twos when unable to create much separation. He also needs to keep developing his feel for making passing reads (0.6 assists vs. 1.4 turnovers in 3SSB). Defensively, he's just as engaged and physical. He's going to be switchable onto bigger players, but not quite as quick as some smaller guards. Overall, he's strong and physical with a high-motor and looks to impose his will on both ends of the floor. As he continues to extend and polish his skill set, he projects as a tough and versatile two-way weapon.

Ament is one of the best long-term prospects in the country. He's an immense talent and still in the early stages of putting it all together. He has good positional size for a face-up forward at over 6-foot-9 with an elastic body type, albeit a relatively modest 6-foot-11 wingspan. He's a fluid mover, plus athlete, and has tremendous natural hands and touch, which are the foundation of his developing skill-set. While not completely polished, Ament shows real potential as both a handler and a shooter. He posted 47/42/90 shooting splits in the 3SSB season. He has a high release and soft touch with range that extends out to the three-point line, and yet is still learning to get consistently balanced in his lower body. The ball hits his hand incredibly softly when he puts it on the floor, and he's starting to show more creativity off the bounce as well. There are even some very real flashes of floor vision and passing ability, with both hands. The most glaring limitation in Ament's game right now is a lack of strength and sheer force. His naturally wiry frame is only just beginning to fill out. He also tends to play a bit upright, raising his center of gravity, which makes it that much easier for him to get bumped or angled off the line of his drive. Right now, Ament can also struggle to finish through contact in a high-level game. Despite that, he shows a real ability to get his own shot off. In addition to that physical maturation, we're also just starting to see the first signs of an alpha mentality in Ament. He's come up the ranks as someone who was content to blend in and rarely looked to really assert himself. Now, we're gradually starting to see him get more assertive, and the expectation is that could come in correlation with the progression of his body. Even defensively, we're seeing him fly around more and make more plays off the ball. What's glaring about Ament is just how much potential he has to get better. He needs to get stronger, keep getting more aggressive, and polish all aspects of his skill set, but all the tools are in place for him to evolve into a skilled and smooth 6-foot-9-plus perimeter forward who is a true three-range scoring threat. The bottom line on Ament is that there may be some five-star prospects who are more capable of dominating a game today, but he has as high a long-term upside as potentially anyone in the class.
