James is an aggressive scoring guard and equally scrappy defender. While he may be slightly undersized, he has solid length with a 6-foot-3 wingspan, and a now chiseled frame. Defensively, his length, quickness, and physical strength are all assets. He also takes pride in his performance on that end of the floor, even beyond what shows up in the stat sheet, and is an ideal starting point for a team’s defensive system. He can pick-up the ball for the length of the floor, get over ball-screens, pressure opposing ball-handlers, and simultaneously beat them to spots. Offensively, he’s added noticeably more burst to his attack in recent years. He plays with pace in the open floor, has a quick first-step in the half-court, and a tight handle that he can breakdown defenders with. James is capable of getting all the way to the rim, but also very adept at creating space for his pull-up game. His three-point shooting showed signs of growth during the high school season, when he knocked down 44% of his attempts, but was inconsistent during the recent EYBL regular season at just 16%.

Pitt is a utility power forward who is long (7-foot wingspan), strong and physical. He plays hard and competes with a very high motor. He’s a high-volume rebounder and consistent double-double threat and also a versatile defender because of his length, mobility, physicality, and sheer effort. He doesn’t need to put up big numbers offensively to impact the game, but he’s capable of scoring on short drives, by turning his left shoulder in the post, or in catch-and-finish situations. He’s also shown some passing potential at times, accumulating 43 assists against just 22 turnovers in EYBL play. While he can make an occasional mid-range shot, he’s not yet a consistent floor spacer, with a release from his deep shoulder, and was a 63% free-throw shooter in the EYBL. Overall, he’s a rugged, blue collar forward that impacts winning without needing to be an offensive focal point.

Elamin has glaring tools and an extremely high upside, but is still working to turn that potential into production on a more consistent basis. He’s extremely smooth with great size. He covers the court with long and fluid strides and has a rare type of elasticity to his frame as a potential jumbo wing. While he’s wiry by nature, with more narrow hips and shoulders, he’s going to have to keep bulking up to play through more contact. While his potential is undeniable, his overall production is still behind. He averaged just under 6 points per game in the EYBL and has played a complimentary role both there and during the high school season. He has tools to develop with the ball though. He has some shooting potential and is a very underrated passer, with both hands. He can put the ball on the floor in the open court, where he is often most intriguing, but is not a traffic handler at this point. His court coverage is major defensive asset, particularly in run-and-jump schemes, while he should be increasingly versatile on that end as he gets stronger. Overall, there are a wide range of potential outcomes for Elamin as his natural tools and talent could ultimately translate up levels, while it all remains dependent on future development.

Jaylen Harrell is a long and broadly built scoring wing who plays primarily off his jumper, but is a threat in various ranges. He has solid positional size and a better-than-plus-four inch wingspan. While he's not a naturally quick or explosive athlete, he's strong, able to get to his spots, and score over top of contesting defenders with a high release. While Harrell has converted 32.6% of his threes in the EYBL regular season, he's a better shooter than that number indicates. Ironically, while he's known as more of a shooter, it's inside the arc where he's most effective, shooting 58% on two-point field goals thanks to the prowess of his pull-up game and willingness to bring smaller, or less physical, defenders into the mid-post area. He can have a high-volume attack at times, but has shown real progression as a passer recently, some signs of being a good off-ball cutter, and also has good hands and natural touch. The biggest area of emphasis will be on the defensive end of the floor, where his lack of footspeed can limit him at times, but he has the length and upper body strength to compensate if he can continue to make gains with his overall conditioning.

Stevens is a big-bodied two-way guard with good size at 6-foot-4 and a strong, 195-pound frame. Offensively, he’s at his best when attacking off the bounce. He’s very dangerous with the ball in the open court, but also has a knack for instinctively making his way through traffic in the half-court. He has the strength and physicality to absorb contact and maintain his angle on the way to the rim, and has started to show more potential to make plays for others as a passer off the dribble. He’s also willing to take smaller guards into the mid-post and exploit mismatches there. Stevens is a capable three-point shooter with room and rhythm, having converted 35% of his attempts from behind the arc in UAA play, but not yet quite as consistent when shooting on the move, with a release point that can drift to the left side of his head. Defensively, Stevens has the potential to be a real difference maker as his size, strength, and movement skills allow him to guard multiple positions and push opposing handlers off their spots, while he also has a really good nose for the ball (he averaged 1.7 steals per game in UAA play). Long-term, he has a chance to develop into a real stopper on that end of the floor. He’s also a very good perimeter rebounder, who averaged 4.5 boards per game in UAA play, and loves to start the break himself.

Holland is a big, power guard. He’s strong and physical with a college ready body and obvious toughness. He has the potential to be a very versatile defender because of his overlap of size, strength, and sheer competitiveness. He already guards multiple positions, gets his hands on a lot of balls, and is even pretty quick off his feet challenging plays at the rim when rotating from the weakside of the floor, which should translate to him being a pretty valuable low man, for a perimeter player, at the next level. Offensively, he’s not unskilled, but could still stand to polish his game with the ball. He’s a shooting threat from behind the arc, but not totally pure with his release (31% 3pt in 3SSB play). He can take on some ballhandling responsibilities at times and has moments where he can drop his body to get downhill a bit and also has a deceleration quality about the way he can slow himself down in the lane, but doesn’t have a dynamic first step. Overall, it will be his physical tools and intangibles that are the basis for his chances to get immediate minutes. He has a clear chance to be an excellent defensive player while continued offensive growth could unlock higher upside outcomes.

Pushing 6-foot-7, Blake more than passes the eye test. He's a long and slender athlete who is a high level mover whether it be laterally or getting north and south. He is a bouncy athlete with both a tremendous first step and good lateral quickness. He is a slightly above average rebounder from the wing and he's got a lot of room to add to his game as a slashing driver. While his jump shot doesn't look bad, it is certainly an area where he has a lot of room to improve. Per Synergy he made only 22% and averaged just .75 points per shot on jumpers in the 2024 EYBL season. Should Lewis find more consistency in that aspect of his game, his value skyrockets.

Fogle is a budding big wing with some skills of a jumbo guard. He's a late-bloomer of sorts who emerged in the second half of his high school career and is still just scratching the surface of his potential. He has terrific perimeter size with long legs, and some early definition to his upper body, but a more naturally narrow frame that will need to keep filling out for the next level. He runs very well, can really cover the court, and has some clear toughness and a high basketball IQ. With Seattle Select, he was utilized as a jumbo creator who could make plays for himself and others while taking good care of the basketball (2.3 assists vs. 1.3 turnovers in UA Rise play). While he wouldn't always have the physicality or first-step to beat his defender cleanly with his initial move, he could nonetheless still handle, pass, and navigate tight spaces with the ball very well for a player his size. At Compass, he's adjusted to new levels of competition without any reluctance and proven the vertical athleticism and physicality to more than hold his own. He rarely shows the same pull-ups and step-backs we saw during the grassroots season, but has addressed some questions about his shooting after converting just 26% of his three-point attempts in UA Rise play with atypical mechanics. He still has unorthodox hand placement as he loads up and leans back into a low release point that can generate some sidespin, but nonetheless shoots it confidently and seems to be reliable as a spot-up floor spacer. Overall, Fogle may need to keep getting stronger and keep proving the consistency of his shooting, but he is still early in the arc of his overall development, has extremely versatile tools, and the approach that makes it easy to bet on his long-term development.

Stagg's rate of improvement over the last year has been off the charts. He's blossomed from a burly big man to an inside-out threat. He is now cut-up and defined, but still powerful, and much more explosive athletically because of it. He's still broad and strong, but not so much that it limits his mobility. Stagg is an emphatic finisher, especially when he has a step to elevate, and also an increasingly effective rim protector who blocked 1.8 shots per game in the recent EYBL season. He runs the floor with similarly powerful strides, has soft hands, and a solid left hand as well. While Stagg has made great physical strides, his face-up skill set has also started to evolve in a significant way. He's now starting to show potential both as a rhythm shooter and when he puts the ball on the floor. The key here though is being able to expand his game without becoming less efficient. He clearly wants to play facing the basket now and has moments where he can try to do too much. Similarly, Stagg can settle for fade-aways and find himself off balance more than a player with his body type should. Defensively, Stagg is active, plays with a motor, and has versatile potential. He can body up with bigger players inside and is effective in sliding his feet laterally, but can still find himself slightly out of position at times. While he rates as a very good rim protector, his defensive rebounding numbers weren't quite as high-volume as expected, both in the EYBL Scholastic and EYBL seasons. Stagg is very young for his grade, and won't turn 18 until the end of August 2025. Given that and his rapid rate of improvement, it's very likely that his best basketball is still far in front of him. He's a physical specimen who could blossom into a highly versatile two-way force.

Mullins proved himself to be one of the best long-range shooters in the country this spring and summer. He knocked down 47% of his attempts from three in 3SSB play, even on high volume, making 6+ threes per game. He has a fairly low, but still quick, release, that he doesn't even always fully extend on. He's a threat to make tough shots in transition, off screens, or the dribble. He also has very deep range, well beyond the college arc at this point. He complements his shooting ability with a good overall feel for the game. He's a solid passer within the flow of the offense, increasingly dangerous cutter, and the type of off ball weapon that coaches will run plays for at the next level. While his niche is behind the arc, he was extremely efficient around the lane too, shooting 60% on two-point field goals, and yet could get more aggressive punishing defenders for running him off the line. Mullins has good positional size at right around 6-foot-5. He's a solid enough athlete to get up and dunk hard with only a step or two of separation. Physically, his shoulders are a bit narrow, but he's just starting to fill-out his frame and already has clear toughness and confidence about him. The other area of the game in which Mullins is a true standout is on the defensive end. He's quick laterally, very active with his hands (1.4 steals per game), and competitive with a scrappy mentality. He's also not afraid to stick his nose into traffic and pull-out some rebounds, on both ends of the floor (3.6 rebounds per game). The bottom line is that when you have an elite shooter, capable of getting hot at any point, who is also a standout defender, with positional size, solid athleticism, and a competitive mentality….you have an elite national prospect.

Odih is imposing on the perimeter because of his strength, physicality, athleticism, and motor. He plays with an attacking mentality and extreme force on both ends of the floor. He can really standout on the defensive end of the floor. He’s very aggressive guarding the ball, able to apply a lot of pressure, and force turnovers. Even when he gets a bad angle initially, he constantly tries to be physical and fight his way back into the play. He would flourish in up-tempo, pressing systems, but his combination of perimeter size, mobility, and strength allows him to be very versatile and switchable in the half-court as well. He is also an exceptional perimeter rebounder, on both ends of the floor, with a quick second bounce. Offensively he can get downhill in the open court, finish above the rim with explosiveness and power alike, get second chance points, and bully most defenders both off the dribble and by bringing them into the post. He'll even turn his own dribble drives into post-ups at times. He’s also shown some instincts as a cutter. He’s made strides with his shooting and overall ball skills, as well as his understanding of spacing and how to play in offensive structure, but those continue to be areas of potential development. Long-term, he’s going to have immediate value because of his physical tools and how hard he plays, regardless of what his offensive role is. If the skill-set can continue to develop, and he can evolve into a legit big guard, that unlocks some higher upside scenarios.

Jemison is in the midst of a major evolution. He came up the ranks as a hybrid four of sorts, who had clear potential with his projectable frame, athleticism, mobility, good hands, and touch, but was a minimal threat from beyond the arc. This spring though, he was utilized as a wing shooter for the first time, attempting nearly 6 threes per game in EYBL play, and knocking down 37%. Moving forward, Jemison has a chance to evolve into a versatile, multi-positional type of wing forward. If the shooting proves to be consistently translatable, even just as a spot-up floor-spacer, there are legitimate 3-and-D type outcomes that becomes possible. Combine that with his ability to run and jump in the open floor and straight-line drive less mobile defenders in the half-court, and he has the potential to be a versatile scoring threat. While he's potentially multi-dimensional, Jemison isn't a primary creator. He's often a two-bounce player right now who rarely dominates the ball or takes bad shots. So while he's not a natural alpha, he's also ideally suited to blend in around other talented players. Defensively, he's a fluid mover who hedges ball-screens well and should have increasing defensive versatility as he continues to fill-out his frame and begins to fully assert himself on that end of the floor.

Forsythe is a shot-making wing with a powerful body. His niche is behind the three-point line. In fact, he’s close to a specialist who attempted almost 75% of his shots from behind the arc in EYBL play (making 41% of them). He has solid lift into his release, a ton of gravity spotting up around the arc, and is very adept at shooting off a lateral dribble. He’s capable of coming off screens and being a movement shooter, although that’s when his misses start to spray around the rim a bit more. Physically, he has a college ready body with broad shoulders and a strong natural frame, but lacks some athletic pop. He’s a functional dribbler with both hands, but rarely creates separation unless he can bulldoze his way downhill. Similarly, he can struggle to finish when he’s not able to purely overpower people, and made just 36% of his two-point field-goals in EYBL play. His lack of rim pressure is also illustrated by a low free-throw rate, although he’s an excellent free-throw shooter once he gets there (89% on just 1.1 attempts per game). Defensively, he can struggle at times against quicker or more athletic wings, but should have some versatility up the line-up because of his power. He’s also capable of asserting himself a bit more as a wing rebounder. Forsythe also offers a variety of intangibles and “little things” that impact winning. He runs the floor hard and plays within himself. He’s a willing ball-mover and reliable decision-maker who averaged less than 1 turnover per game. Overall, there’s just a level of maturity about the way he carries himself and approaches the game.

Frost is one of the best shooters in high school basketball, who knocked down 45% of threes in UAA play. He's also a specialist who took over 75% of his attempted shots from behind the arc. He's a movement shooter, who excels at getting balanced with his feet set, but he is often best on more condensed cuts at this point. Physically, Frost is powerful, with a thick, yet cut, frame that has obviously been earned in the weight room. But he does have somewhat heavier feet on the defensive end. He's shown some growth getting downhill when run off the line, but lacks elite lift at the rim. Frost is a willing passer and reliable decision-maker (2:1 assist-to-turnover ratio), but he likes to jump pass in the lane and can struggle to throw against his momentum at times. The bottom line is that he's one of the most potent floor-spacers in the class, with shot-making to be weaponized in a team's playbook, but still needs to find more ways to consistently impact a high-level game when his shots aren't falling.

Aristode is a jumbo wing with a ton of physical tools that should ultimately translate into a versatile two-way game. He has terrific positional size at 6-foot-8 and an equally impressive frame. He’s not exceptionally long, with a 6-foot-10 wingspan, but is already strong and physical. He’s a high-level athlete who runs very well, is an explosive leaper, and can play up above the rim with ease. He’s a very talented defender with real potential to be a game-changer on that end of the floor. He can slide his feet laterally, has the strength to absorb contact, and the combination of size and mobility necessary to be versatile and guard multiple positions. He’s even able to provide some weak-side playmaking, averaging nearly 2 stocks per game in EYBL play (1.2 steals and 0.7 blocks). Offensively, his game is a bit of a work in progress. His physical tools are the basis for his best assets as he can make some impressive plays in the open floor, rise-up for big finishes, be a lob threat, and play through contact. He also has a general understanding of how to play in offensive structure. His skill-set was a bit rawer when he initially arrived, but has made consistent strides. His spot-up three is now clean when he’s in rhythm (33% 3pt and 75% FT in the EYBL) and he has good use of both hands around the basket. He handles the ball well enough to be a legit wing, but doesn’t always get the ball out in front of him in the open floor and can be an errant passer on the move. He puts pressure on the rim at times, but doesn’t get downhill quite as much as someone with his body type should. While Aristode’s physical tools, and even his recent improvements, are undeniable, there have also been moments where his body language and overall motor have been inconsistent. Maintaining that competitive consistency within his daily approach is obviously a key to maximizing both his current productivity and long-term potential, particularly for a player who is perhaps most naturally gifted on the defensive end of the floor.

Singleton was one of the biggest stock-risers in the national 2025 class this summer. While he may not be a prototypical high-major athlete in terms of his quickness or leaping ability, he's an efficient inside-out threat and is consistently productive. At 6-foot-8 with naturally broad shoulders, Singleton is strong, able to play through contact, and capable of playing either the four or the five. What he may lack in terms of sheer burst, he compensates for with outstanding balance through contact and an associated ability to make tough lay-ups in traffic. He has excellent footwork at various spots on the floor and very good hands. That helps him not just offensively, but also on the glass. Singleton has some mismatch skills with his ability to make spot-up shots out to the three-point line and put the ball on the floor with both hands. Singleton is also a quality passer who has good instincts and an overall feel for the game. This will allow him the potential to be someone teams can play through in the half-court. Defensively, there are times when Singleton's lack of foot speed can be a limitation, but he relies on strength and intellect to get ideal angles on that end of the floor. While he plays bigger than he is inside and is best utilized defending opposing bigs, he is not a prototypical rim protector. It is vital that Singleton maximizes his potential to finish possessions as a high-volume rebounder. Overall, he may not be the most mobile, agile, or explosive, but he projects as a reliable team defender.

Simcoe is a southpaw forward who is skilled, tough, and very smart. At 6-foot-8 with broad shoulders and a strong frame, he has good positional size and a college-ready body. He's not a creator or high-volume scorer, but he fits within offensive structure because he can make open spot-up shots out to the three-point line, put the ball on the floor in either direction to attack a close-out or play off a shot-fake, and facilitate with his passing, patience, and ability to make reads. Simcoe is not a bad vertical athlete, especially when he has space to elevate off of two feet. He's fully engaged defensively and consistently willing to give effort, but better suited to defend inside than out. He's strong and physical, even when bodying up with bigger buys in the post, thus allowing his team to play smaller line-ups. On the perimeter though, he's less fluid laterally, and a bit stiff in his hips. His rebounding numbers were fairly limited in the UAA (3.2 per game), but he was the type who consistently blocked out and prevented his man from getting the ball, and has shown an ability to rebound in much higher volume during the high school season. Overall, Simcoe be never be an alpha type scorer, but his combination of skill, physical strength, positional size, effort, and intellect should help him impact winning sooner rather than later at the next level.

Virtually all of Hines' high school scouting report remains valid today. If there was a surprise, it was that his transition to the college level was as seamless as it was, even after playing summer grassroots basketball. Perhaps the other surprise was that he didn't get more than 18 minutes per game, given how productive he was. He led the Big East with 2.2 blocks per game, ripped down 2.4 offensive rebounds, and shot 60% from the floor. He was a bit foul prone because of his hard-charging, physical style, and still isn't much of a shooting or playmaking threat away from the basket. Nevertheless, he has a chance to develop into one of the more impactful interior bigs in the country.

Sanders is a big and strong lefty guard who played almost exclusively with the ball in his hands this spring and summer. He plays with an attacking mentality and creates all types of mismatch problems for opposing guards who can't contend with his size and strength. Sanders can both get downhill to put pressure on the rim, and also has a terrific change of pace off the dribble with the body control to maintain his balance through contact. He can be very crafty getting paint touches and playing in attack mode, without getting sped up, and knows how to both get himself to the free-throw line and create for others. Sanders is also an excellent rebounding guard who loves to take it off the glass and immediately push the ball to start the break. He can also rise-up for some loud dunks. Defensively, he's equally competitive and physically aggressive, with definite potential to guard multiple positions at the next level. While Sanders can stuff all columns of the stat sheet (18.7 points, 5.5 rebounds, and 4.3 assists in the UA Rise), he needs the ball in his hands to do it, and can also be inefficient at times (38% FG). He's not a natural shooter and made just 23% of his three-point attempts, despite having the freedom to attempt almost five threes per game. It remains to be seen if his game translates into being a true big lead guard at the next level, or if he's better utilized as an ultimate utility guard, when surrounded by better players, where he can likely play and defend all three perimeter positions. It's also notable that while he looks fully mature, he's young for his grade, meaning there could be an increased chance for untapped upside.

Sylla proved to be the exact type of mobile and athletic big man and defensive game changer that we expected when he first committed to Georgia Tech. He pulled down 7.2 rebounds and 2.0 stocks (1.2 blocks and 0.8 steals) in just 21.4 minutes per night. That 6.6% block rate ranked in the 96th percentile nationally while his 2.0% steal rate was in the 60th percentile. Most impressive though was his 99th percentile as a defensive rebounder, and it's the overlap of the shot-blocking and defensive rebounding that makes him so rare. While he has a knack for protecting the rim, he doesn't sacrifice his defensive rebounding position to do it. Combine that with the mobility to ultimately be a versatile frontcourt defender, and he has a wealth of natural tools on that end of the floor. Offensively, he's as raw as expected, but was still able to utilize his athleticism to get nearly 2 dunks per game, albeit with a puzzling habit of missing dunks. His first step was a weapon at times as well, utilizing it to blow past less mobile bigs when he didn't have to put the ball on the floor more than once. His activity on the offensive glass was also a major asset. Otherwise though, his offense was limited. He was not a scoring threat away from the basket, his turnovers more than doubled his assists, and his 43% shooting from the free-throw line was very concerning. Sylla also only played 16 games last season. He opened the year with double-doubles in five of his first six games, but injured his ankle in December and surprisingly barely returned to the rotation after that, leading to public speculation about his durability and/or commitment.

Rosario was one of the biggest national stock-risers in the spring of 2025 before subsequently reclassifying to commit to Kansas. With a strong, college ready, frame, and a good skill-set he will provide immediate value with his shooting and floor-spacing. While the three-point shooting is probably his best asset, he’s also going to be able to attack close-outs, straight line-drive, play through contact, and elevate off two-feet. He isn’t quite as quick laterally though and so there are some concerns about how he’ll adapt on the defensive end of the floor. At this point, without much creativity or change of direction in his handle, he’s probably best described as a wing, where he lacks ideal positional size at 6-foot-4.

Isaiah Johnson was one of the most pleasant surprises in the national freshman class this year, going from an unranked three-star prospect to someone who cracked the starting line-up for Colorado mid-year and ended up averaging 17 points, 3 assists, and 3 rebounds per game while shooting 49% from the floor, 38% from the three-point line, and 82% from the free-throw line, while only turning the ball over 1.4 times in 30.6 minutes per night. The lefty has a versatile attack, but his most natural gifts may be his burst and twitch as a playmaker. He's a constant threat to get a piece of the paint and most efficient offensively when he's in the open floor or coming off a ball-screen. While he lacks overwhelming size or strength, he's also a very efficient lay-up maker, rating in the 87th percentile per Synergy. He's a good enough three-point shooter to punish opposing defenders for playing him as a driver, with similarly reliable accuracy off the catch or the dribble. He also has a fairly analytically driven shot chart, with the majority of his looks coming at the rim or behind the arc. He even took very good care of the basketball for a freshman lead guard Physically, we'll see if his lack of size or strength becomes problematic, or limits his ability to switch.

Onyejiaka is a major physical presence in the lane. He's long, strong, aggressive, and just physically dominant inside the lane, primarily as a finisher, rebounder, post defender, and rim protector. He's close to maxed out physically though and needs to continue to polish his game. He misses a surprising amount of dunks for someone so physically dominant, isn't much of a scoring threat on the perimeter, and can lack solutions when not able to overpower the opposition.

Witherspoon is a bigger-bodied guard with backcourt size, a naturally broader frame, huge hands and feet. He's not a dynamic athlete but creates mismatches with his size, physicality, and well-rounded skill set. When he's at his best, he's making shots from three, which can open up the rest of his attack. His mechanics are generally clean, although he can follow through down on his release a bit, and he's a threat to make deep threes when he gets it going. But his shooting inconsistencies (26% from three-point range on almost six attempts per game in the UAA) and high turnover rate (3 assists vs. 3.3 turnovers) can make him inefficient at times. Witherspoon lacks the first step to get by most set defenders cleanly, and frequently requires a second move. So, while he has some on/off ball versatility, he's at his best on the second side of the floor – where the defense has already been in motion and he can overpower smaller guards as a physical driver, be a secondary pick-and-roll threat, or attack a close-out. He's rarely an above-the-rim finisher, but still fairly efficient in the lane with his body control and ability to contort his frame as a finisher (53% 2pt FG vs. 41% FG overall). Defensively, Witherspoon is competitive, physical, and has good hands (2.0 steals per game), but not especially quick. He's a gritty quarter-court defender and switchable up the line-up, but more susceptible to being beaten by quicker guards when too far extended or pulled away from his help. He's also a valuable rebounding guard (4.2 rebounds per game).

Amir Jenkins is a playmaking lead guard and tone-setter on both ends of the floor who is loaded with competitive intangibles. Offensively, he is a constant threat to get a piece of the paint and create for both himself and others. He's dynamic in the open court with great speed, but also creative in the half-court with a quick first-step. Beyond his sheer burst, he has plenty of complimenting weapons at his disposal. His handle is tight so he can break down his defender with combination moves or sharp changes in direction once his attack has begun. He changes speeds, has quick instincts that allow him to improvise as defenders rotate, and is capable of passing off the dribble at any point. Physically, he has solid positional size for a point guard and while he is primary under the rim, he is a skilled lay-up maker. He's going to need to keep getting stronger, but has some deceptive physicality and ability to hold his spots through contact. The shooting is another area that will need to keep making strides. He's now a consistent threat with his mid-range pull-up, but less reliable from three, often leaning back into his release and making just 28% during 3SSB play this spring. Specifically, it's the ability to hit catch-and-shoot threes that will need to grow in order for defenders to not help off him without the ball at the next level. Defensively, he's the type of player you can build around. He's quick enough to pick-up for the length of the floor, has a great nose for the ball, and real competitive energy. That's representative of his total approach as he's loaded with energy and leadership abilities, which is a big part of why he's built such a winning resume over his high school career.

Calderon is a high-level athlete with exceptional length, huge hands, and a frame that is both strong and defined. He’s measured at 6-foot-8 with shoes on with a massive 7-foot-3 wingspan and 10-inch hands. His athletic testing numbers are equally impressive with a 38-inch vertical and the fasted full court sprint time in the entire OTE program. His physical tools are just as impressive within the flow of the game as he has easy bounce, covers the court, and has all the tools necessary to thrive on the defensive end of the floor. Positional versatility should be another big asset for Calderon. He’s definitely going to be capable of defending multiple positions and thus be very switchable. Theoretically, he should also be able to thrive on that end of the floor regardless of tempo, whether that be the half-court or more aggressive run-and-jump type systems. Offensively, he’s undoubtedly going to be at his best in an up-tempo style where he can rely on his athleticism to get easy points in the open floor. He’s not much of a ball-handler or creator at this point, and while he shows some shooting potential with a clean and high release, his shooting percentages have proven consistently unreliable to date, with some occasionally bad misses. Calderon will turn 20 years old in December of 2024, making him about two years older than most high school seniors in the class of 2025. So, while he will be uniquely ready to play from a physical perspective as a college freshman, he’s already close to a finished product in that regard and will have untapped upside almost solely through the progression of his ball skills.

He is a big guard who is really skilled with a high IQ for the game. He’s not the most athletic but he’s a kid who knows how to get to his spots. He is a capable scorer at all three-levels and is very comfortable operating out of ball screens and has some playmaking ability. He is a high floor guy who can come and make an immediate contribution and has the potential to be a multiple year starter.

Furphy is a highly skilled and extremely cerebral facilitating type of wing. He's an ideal connector who thrives in offensive structure with his ability to shoot from long range, make reads, and high-level passes. He has definite gravity as a floor-spacer but is also dangerous as a secondary pick-and-roll option. He's physically strong and not a terrible vertical athlete, but not especially quick laterally. His offense is widely considered to be ahead of his defense at this point, but he's tough and competitive with untapped upside as he makes his conditioning more of a priority.

Measuring in at 6-foot-10 (without shoes) with a 7-foot-2 wingspan, Fru is a long and versatile frontcourt piece who will be turn 22 years old in August before his freshman year at Louisville. On film, he shows offensive value as a finisher as well as flashes of floor-spacing potential. He's a willing ball-mover and doesn't need to be a focal point to make an impact. Defensively, his size and length are clear weapons while he appears to have real potential to be versatile on that end, both from a positional standpoint and guarding ball-screens in various ways.

Avdalas' combination of perimeter size and passing ability is rare, if not unique, in the college basketball ranks. The 6-foot-9 native of Greece arrived at Virginia Tech with legit one-and-done buzz that was only amplified when he went for 33 points in his second game of the season, against Providence. Ultimately though, that early game proved to be his best of the season as he struggled to live up to the hype. Avdalas is every bit as good a passer as advertised, with a versatile array of deliveries in his bag and the ability to see over top of the defense. Where he struggled though was in creating the type of separation to open up passing angles. His inconsistencies as a shooter, knocking down just 31% of his threes and 67% of his free-throws, only exacerbated his inability to get dominoes falling and ultimately led to turnovers and forced shots. While Avdalas can operate as a jumbo initiator when he's playing in space and structure, he's going to struggle to defend opposing lead guards. He's just not a naturally explosive athlete. Consequently, he's ideally suited next to an undersized scoring guard, so that they can play to their natural strengths offensively and cross-match on the defensive end of the floor.

The seven-footer turned 21 mid-way through his freshman year at Arizona State. Nonetheless, he still has plenty of untapped upside, despite posting nearly 14 points, 6 rebounds, and over 2 blocks per game in roughly 30 minutes per night. In addition to his massive size, he's long with a measured 7-foot-4 wingspan, giving him a terrific catch radius around the basket. He owns soft natural touch and developing skill, making 72% of his free-throws and even eight threes on the season. He also has a floater game that he can utilize off short-rolls. He can even put the ball on the floor a bit, although there's some unpredictability and correlating turnovers when he tries to do too much. Defensively, he has a 7.7 block percentage and has a bit more mobility than most seven-footers, but needs to finish possessions better on the defensive glass. The bottom line is that he was one of the most pleasant surprises in last year's national freshman class, and although he's older, there's still a substantial runway for him to continue to improve.

Vaaks is a tremendous offensive talent with a glaring overlap of size and skill. The Estonia native put up 16 points and 3 assists per game as a 20-year-old freshman for Providence, albeit with an inefficient style. He shot just 40% from the field and 35% from behind the arc, while taking over two-thirds of his total shots from three (more than 8 per game). He has the type of fluid release and feathery touch that makes it look like the ball is going in whenever it leaves his hands though, so the expectation is that he should have greater efficiency with just a bit more confined role. He was particularly adept as a pull-up shooter, ranking 12th nationally with 1.3 per game. He even shows some playmaking ability on-the-ball, utilizing his size and ball skills. Increased efficiency will be a by-product of better shot selection and perhaps just a little less freedom, but the biggest concern is on the defensive end. He has yet to make a consistent commitment on that end of the floor, and is particularly vulnerable when asked to match-up with quicker guards in space. Similarly, he needs to make a more consistent commitment to his body and conditioning. He's also not the perimeter rebounder he should be at his size.

Great length as a combo forward at a long 6-8. Is thin now but has the frame to add body mass. More of a smooth athlete than an explosive athlete but can make plays in traffic. Will do more of that with added strength and added mental toughness. Has tremendous body control and can stop on a dime for his shot and also make improvising moves to finish. Has a great shooting touch. In process of developing a more consistent deep range shot. Quite adept at finding the midrange shot. Loves to shoot going left, especially with a hop back move or spin move that direction. Also, a good finisher around the rim. Handles the ball well in space. Can get too high with the dribble in traffic. Sees the court and will deliver the pass on the move. Needs to up his intensity on the defensive end. Has a feel for blocking shots. Often, however, plays too upright and without enough strength at the point of attack. Has great hands as a rebounder. Developing strength will certainly aide him as a rebounder. Has a ton of potential. Key is tightening up his skills and his mental toughness while gaining weight and strength. More Evaluations Evaluated 04/02/2020 Evan Daniels Director of Basketball Recruiting Read

Has a lean frame with good size and length for position. Has strong shot mechanics with a smooth stroke and clean, quick release. Capable of making shots on the move, bounce or off catch. Shows good footwork and deep range. Not just a shooter; has runners/floaters in arsenal. Passes very well for position. Could stand to improve athletically and get stronger while also improving his handle. Has high floor and projects as a multi-year starter for Harvard.

Score-first wing with good size and long arms. Projectable frame with plenty of room to gain strength. Can score from all three levels. Most comfortable scoring with jumper but can get to basket as well. Has formidable weapon from midrange with his floater. Efficiency as scorer could improve with better shot selection; tends to hunt shots at times. Size and length give him upside on defense. Projects as high major starter.

At around 6-foot-7, doesn’t fit into the traditional mold of a wing or a power forward. A tough player who just produces. Can hit 3-pointers, though that isn’t yet a strength. At his best going off the dribble and making plays using his athleticism. Needs to continue to refine his skill set, but the motor and toughness make him a difficult matchup for opposing defenders. Could make an NBA rosters, but likely won't be drafted.

Moore has fine size at just over 6-5, but he has a massive wingspan that stretches to 7 feet. A defensive minded wing prospect, Moore is a good athlete that plays the game with toughness and physicality. He is loaded with potential on the defensive end, and he can guard multiple positions. Gives great effort on that side of the ball. Offensively, Moore is at his best as a slasher. He finishes well at the rim and is particularly good in transition. He’s a fairly good passer. The next step is developing as a shooter/scorer. He has shown signs, but he has to get much more consistent shooting from 3-point range. Moore is a multi-year college prospect.

White is a talented and smooth two-guard with ideal size for the position. White has the feel and athleticism to create space and score in any situation. He needs to make his jumper more of a consistent weapon but has the length, athleticism, and feel to develop into a solid scoring option for a high major program.

Has a top notch physique at 6-7. Powerful athlete. Son of a former NFL linebacker. Controls horizontal space. Quick off the floor vertically. Wants to attack the basket and finish but is also adept at creating scoring opportunities for his teammates. Is a keep the defense honest three-point shooter with somewhat of a mechanical release. Partly because of his physical strength, handles the ball with composure and sees the court. Has a feel for the developing pass and can accurately deliver it. Defensively can guard all five positions. Is a highly capable rebound. Doesn't overwhelm in any one particular area of the game, but is solid across the board, plays with intelligence and composure, and has imposing powerful athleticism.

Murray is a lengthy combination forward who has the tools to potentially become a versatile asset for a successful program. A very raw prospect, as of now he is more of a small-ball four than a true wing because his ball skills are still in its developing stages, and he has a high level of comfort playing in the high post. He impacts the game offensively with his feel as a passer on his attack. A strong right-hand driver, Murray has the natural ability to find the open man while looking to slash and score. He is most impactful defensively with his activity while guarding on or off the ball. Murray still has ways to go as far as adding weight to his frame and overall strength to limit turnovers and uncomfortable shot attempts, but he projects as a impactful who can leave an imprint on a game in different ways.

Has positive length for a 6-7 wing player. Gets out in transition and finishes well. Thin frame at this point but plays through contact. Quick leaper off the floor and has quality body control. Has an athletic and accurate shooting stroke from deep. Can make tough threes off the dribble and off the move on the catch. Has a dangerous pull up jumper and can get to the rim. Has a solid and functional handle and will find open teammates. Rebounding should improve with added strength. Is a disruptor on defense with ability to get into passing lanes. Is a multi positional defender.

A highly skilled guard, Friedrichsen is an excellent three-point shooter, but more versatile scoring threat than he gets credit for. He can come off pin-downs and staggers, make shots off the dribble, and deep threes as well. He’s also a threat in the mid-range area with a good knack for creating space and a natural fade on his release that helps him get it off. He’s fundamentally sound with a good feel for the game and high basketball IQ. His frame is still undeveloped physically at this point, but with an excellent foundation of skill and basketball acumen, his game should continue to evolve at a rapid rate in the coming years as his body begins to catch-up. More Evaluations Evaluated 03/27/2021 Brandon Jenkins Recruiting Analyst Read

Sutton is a big lefty wing who is a high-level athlete with a rangy and elastic build. He's capable of making explosive plays in the open floor, has clear slashing potential with a good first-step, a solid right hand, and under-rated passing ability. The most notable limitation in his offensive game right now is his perimeter shooting. His natural touch isn't bad, but he brings the ball across his body in his release and often shoots it with sidespin. Defensively, there's clear upside because he can really move his feet and run, and also possesses the athleticism to recover even when he's initially out of position, but needs to be more disciplined about getting low in his stance. Overall, Sutton may be older for his grade, but he's still in the early stages of his overall development. He has clear tools and a high-ceiling, but is going to require patience and consistent development.

Passmore is a strong and athletic wing. He’s a major threat in transition where he runs the floor hard and can rise-up for the type of explosive finishes that can immediately change the momentum of a game. He’s a downhill straight-line driver, who doesn’t have a ton of wiggle to his game, but can go through contact. He’s made strides as a shooter and has become capable from three with room and rhythm. Passmore is also a projectable defender with a real chance to become a weapon on that end of the floor because he can get low in his stance, slide his feet laterally, and absorb contact in his upper body.

Hill is a scoring guard and tough shot-maker who can put points on the board in bunches. He went from a seldomly utilized role player as a freshman under Ryan Odom to the Atlantic 10's Most Improved Player and 6th Man of the Year under Phil Martelli, and then the hero of VCU's NCAA Tournament win over North Carolina. He ended up averaging 15 points and nearly 3 assists per game on 47/37/84 shooting, all while committing just 1.1 turnover per game. Hill complements his multi-level shot-making prowess with a tight and creative handle. He has deep range, extreme gravity (51% on unguarded catch-and-shoot threes), the ability to make tough shots with minimal separation (36% on contested catch-and-shoot threes), a lethal mid-range pull-up game (57% on 2pt jumpers off the dribble), and even sneaky good efficiency in the paint for a below-the-rim finisher (63% on lay-ups). He'll rock defenders with his handle, looking for step-backs, but being prepared to take advantage of lanes to the rim. He's also a capable passer, particularly as a lob thrower, who takes good care of the ball despite thriving with a lot of freedom. Physically, he has solid size, particularly when he's on the ball, but lacks standout strength or athleticism.

Enormous pass catcher with elite height and catch radius who possesses the frame to become a true tight end, but the functional athleticism on the perimeter to play wideout. Basketball player whose hardwood acumen regularly manifests on the gridiron. Excellent body control and adjustment ability, especially for such a large athlete. Natural high pointer who's a devastating jump-ball target. Size, hand strength and dexterity, athleticism, and timing allow for highlight-reel, in-traffic grabs. Near 240 pounds prior to senior season, but seemed to trim down a bit in Fall 2023 and showed impressive suddenness and athleticism overall. Still honing route-running craft, but displays encouraging ability to leverage defensive backs in the short-to-intermediate passing game. Developing consistent late separation will be key against high-major defenders, but catch window is quite large and combines with elite physical tools to essentially make him an open target even when seemingly covered up. Is he giant outside receiver, a matchup tight end, or a true, in-line tight end after gaining weight? Don't overthink it. One of the nation's most intriguing offensive prospects in the 2024 class, possessing virtually limitless long-term potential with a developmental ceiling in the NFL Draft's higher rounds. Former jumbo wideout Plaxico Burress and Rams tight end Tyler Higbee represent pros with traits similar to Green.

Able is a well-built wing with broad shoulders and a very sturdy frame that is only going to keep filling out. He has somewhat unorthodox mechanics for someone who makes threes in bunches, with just a slight hop into a noticeable twisting motion, but nevertheless a fluid release and soft ball. While the jumper looked like the first domino in his attack, he showed the versatility to also make mid-range shots off the dribble and solid athleticism in the open floor. The other thing that was very noteworthy, was Able’s excellent left hand. He was equally comfortable, not just putting the ball on the floor to both sides, but even dunking with both hands. He also showed good perimeter footwork off the catch and was a competitive defender.

Has length for a wing at 6-6 and is very likely still growing. Is a smooth athlete but only relatively explosive and limited laterally. Has a smooth shooting stroke and deep range. Can be crafty with the dribble. Average passer. Will rebound his position. Needs to improve strength and lateral quickness.

Has length as a wing with a wingspan pushing 7-feet. Not an overly explosive athlete but a good athlete. Great body control and can make plays in traffic. Good outside shooter who can score at all three levels. Gets to the rim well with straight-line drives off the shot fake. Also has an effective crossover dribble. Solid ball handler but more of a scorer than a distributor. Rebounds his position and is a dangerous offensive rebounder. Not the quickest player laterally but is a smart defensive player who knows how to take advantage of his length

Wright is one of the most offensively gifted lead guards in all of college basketball. He can toggle back and forth between operating as a pure point guard and having spurts where he's aggressively looking for his own offense. His sweet spot comes when he strikes a balance. Wright has burst into the lane, savvy changing speeds, and a good understanding of how to use his body. He's also a talented distributor, when he so chooses, who had three games with 9 or more assists as a sophomore. After being an inconsistent shooter at times in high school, he made 41% of his threes last year at BYU, ranking in the 96th percentile on all catch-and-shoot attempts per Synergy, but not being quite as accurate off the dribble. Wright is undersized, rates only as an average finisher, and can have some defensive limitations as well.

Jeffrey is an ultra-competitive and consistently productive power guard. He stuffs a stat sheet like few other guards in the country and averaged 14 points, 5 rebounds, 5 assists, and almost 2 steals per game during the EYBL season. He has good backcourt size at 6-foot-3, a very strong build, and plays with both a high-motor and extreme physicality. His intangibles and leadership skills are off the charts as he sets the tone on both ends of the floor. While he’s not a conventional point guard, he’s often played with the ball in his hands. He’s capable of initiating offense, but can be turnover prone when asked to be the primary handler. He excels at getting downhill, playing through contact to get paint touches, and snapping kick-out passes once he collapses the lane. He’s also comfortable stopping for mid-range pull-ups, but an inconsistent three-point shooter with questionable, set-shot type, mechanics (30% 3pt in EYBL while averaging just one made three every two games). Jeffrey is a very aggressive defender who gets after people on that end of the floor and is capable of forcing turnovers. His physical strength also gives him the versatility to switch onto bigger players, while he is also an excellent rebounding guard. Overall, he may not have the most polished skill-set or a ton of wiggle, but he’s the type of player who raises the level of everyone around him and is consistently productive because of his physicality, toughness, motor, and aggressive two-way floor game.

The younger brother of Max Christie, Cam has similar perimeter size and body type, but a different type of game at the same stage. His best individual offense comes in the mid-range area with his pull-up, and he knows how to get to it using his dribble, various fakes, and good footwork. He is a smart player who generally knows how to play, handles and passes well, and can even facilitate from various spots on the floor in the half-court. He needs to keep progressing as a spot-up shooting threat with a release that is less fluid from that range at this point. Christie is not a totally conventional player. He’s multi-faceted and multi-dimensional, consequently able to create various mismatches on the perimeter, and at his best when utilized accordingly. However, if his body keeps emerging and he develops his jump-shot, his game could go to another level altogether.

Ward is coming off a hugely productive EYBL season in which he averaged over 20 points, 7 rebounds, and 2 assists per game on 56% shooting from the floor, after embracing more of an inside-out style. He has sturdy size for a college four-man at 6-foot-7 with shoes on and adds very good length with a 6-foot-11 wingspan. His frame is solid, particularly in his lower body where he has long and strong legs. The upper body will need to continue to add muscle mass in the coming years. While Ward's game does not yet extend beyond 15 feet with any type of consistency (he was just 4-for-32 from three in EYBL play), he has a versatile attack from the mid-range and in. His first step isn't dynamic, but he puts pressure on the rim in short drive situations (especially when he's able to catch and rip), gets off his feet pretty quickly as a finisher, and gets himself to the free-throw line in high volume because he goes right into contact. Ward can post mismatches and shows some floor vision and passing ability with his back to the basket, even throwing crosscourt skips. While he may want to expand his handle and shooting range in future years, right now Ward is hugely efficient inside the arc with 62% shooting on two-point field goals. Defensively too, he is more naturally suited to defend fours than threes, but he's plenty physical and a high-volume rebounder who can finish possessions next to a rim-protecting five. Ward is also a coach's son who competes with consistent energy, understands the game, and appears to be a good teammate. The bottom line is that when Ward embraces being a four-man, he's a hugely productive and efficient as both a scorer and rebounder.

What stands out about Codie is that he fits the physical profile of a modern day four man. He can really run the floor; he moves pretty well laterally and he has the ability to play facing the basket. No. he’s not yet a prolific shooter from deep, but he does make enough from beyond the three-point line to keep defenders honest. He is also starting to develop the ability to attack of the dribble. Around the rim he is able to use his quickness and length to his advantage, he has good anticipation as a rebounder and good hands. He’s a quick leaper who can catch and finish lobs and while he’s not your traditional help side shot blocker, he is capable of providing some rim protection. The biggest keys for Codie will be that he really embraces the weight room, keeps refining his overall skill package and then figures out to best way to marry his ability to play both inside and facing the rim. As the son of a former pro athlete, he’s been able to grow up understanding what it takes to succeed at the highest level.

Good size for four man. Sturdy frame even with just OK length. Solid base with room to gain strength. Can hit catch-and-shoot 3-pointers but best offensively from midrange and closer. Efficient midrange shooter who can take bigger defenders off dribble. Has plus vision for position. Impressive instincts as shot blocker and rebounder. Needs to add strength, improve ball skills and work on shot selection. Has upside to move up but with normal progression likely falls into second round range. Should be quality high major starter regardless.
